The fact that really piss-poor, incompetent art in the cuddly kitten and badly painted playboy nude categories are even covered in this magazine really bugs me! I don't mind art that is done by people with a different world view from mine--no matter how strange it appears. It is the stuff that is purely commercial--that the artist knows is crap and that the galleries that sell it know is crap--that really bothers me! I know that the editors of this magazine also know that it is crap. They just know that it sells and that there are buyers for it. To me that is not an excuse. top_profane.text: I am only reviewing issues from 1 to 30 of Nintendo Power. Back then, I do remember them as I subscribed to the magazine every year for a few years. Then, I lost interest because the magazine was making a transition from NES to SNES. The biggest positive feature about the magazine is how colorful the artwork (at times, became too much while the actual screenshots of the games were too little) was. The worst feature of it was the paucity of information I was getting about the games that I actually played on. Let's not forget back then that NES games were too expensive to buy, even with the lowered salaries our parents actually earned. There were too many games mentioned in Nintendo Power that it was impossible for any average kid to own many of them. Needless to say, Nintendo Power became a huge shameless marketing tool to try to persuade the kid readers to succumb to their temptations and get their parents to give in to buy the games for them. Now, after 20 years, I've finally a look at the old issues and see what I currently think of them. First off, the writers did very well in explaining the basic moves for certain games. However, they didn't reveal very much about advanced moves. Sometimes, they didn't bother showing the readers other features of the game. It was just a load of irrelevant artwork. For example, in Super Mario Bros. 2, there was no illustration of using coins to pull the lever for slot machine at the end of each stage. Looking at the first issue, they did a very good job in explaining a lot of details and including a full scale of maps of each level so you can see what comes next throughout. But, why did they stop covering any more after a certain level and never look at the final two or three stages? A great example is Issue 1 where they covered Super Mario Bros. 2 from worlds 1-1 to 2-3 and just abruptly stopped thereafter. Same thing goes for Gauntlet where there were 100 rooms to explore, but only three were covered in Nintendo Power. In Issue 10 which was among the worst, there was so little information about Batman and Double Dragon 2 that the coverages became a joke. They didn't even try to explain what happens during a fight with Bosses. And there was nothing about the late stages of the games. Come on, that's all they got? Contra got absolutely weak coverage too despite its popularity. There is a lot of times that this happens, and it makes me to think that the writers actually never got that far or have not beaten these games. Moreover, the writers have exaggerated a lot about the features of the games (many of them were in fact terrible) which supports the notion that there was a clear bias involved. Looking at Nintendo advertisement where you use the Powerline to the Pros by calling them and then noticing in the table of contents where it says, "Our pros can get you out of any video jam." why establish the phone line already when it should be published in the magazine? Is that the magazine's goal to help you out on that because you paid for the subscription to it? Bah...they want to make more money off from the readers. On page 54 of Issue 1, what a weak advice about how to beat Mike Tyson. They can do better than that. Reading a lot of the advices elsewhere, Nintendo Power seems to be more interested in giving out cheat codes rather than instructing the readers in how to beat a game the pure way. Why was there information about movies, music, and celebrities who happen to play Nintendo? The magazine was supposed to be about the games of NINTENDO! What a waste of space. Then, there is a silly page of NES ACHIEVERS where players were recognized for their highest scores per game (um...Game Genie, yes? Of course, it came out in early 1990, making cheating very plausible; plus Nintendo Power never endorsed it due to supposed copyright infringement). Really, beating the game was, and still is today, always the ultimate goal, and nobody cared about points. Even a worse embarrassment was the recognition of achievements by "Power Players."  Time to get out of the house and smell the roses. What's with this Nester and the smug Howard Phillips? Get rid of them already! Why do they show crappy made up stories too? Can we stick with strategies of NES games? Every time I pick up an issue, it's always Zelda, Super Mario 2, Zelda, Metroid, Zelda, Super Mario 3, MegaMan, Super Mario 2, Super Mario 3, Zelda, Super Mario 3, etc. These games got the most coverage of any. Most unfair of them all, there was a 85 page issue (number 13) that was entirely devoted to Super Mario 3. Some of the other games received so little of coverage, and the rest had none (I was really surprised about the lack of coverage for The Simpsons: Bart vs. the Space Mutants) at all. You were out of luck if you were playing any of them because you were trying to figure out how to get past a certain point. Most likely, whichever the company paid the magazine the most got the most coverage. I still can't get over the sight of the dork in white jersey titled Nintendo Power every time I see him and his goofy hair. Damn, issue 5, pg. 19. features what may be the worst accessory of all time in NES history...the Power Glove (That commercial was very convincing. By the way, what happened to the review of the Super Glove Ball game in Nintendo Power?). Let us not forget the wonderful toy called U-Force. They really made it sound so good. The shameless promotion of crappy NES accessories was embarrassing; it would have been nicer if they could have admitted the truth that these accessories were in fact unworkable. However and interestingly, both of the accessories including some others were never mentioned again in future issues (sort of got swept under the rug quietly). Elsewhere, the PowerMeter was the most biased and worthless feature of the magazine because all of the games were thought of so highly and possessed no negative marks, but we all know after getting them, we recognized how truly crappy these games were. Some of the advices the "counselors" (what a ridiculous title) gave for certain games have been questionable at best, and there are certainly far easier solutions than them. One of the "counselors" claimed to have beaten Contra with one life left by using a Power Glove (either it is true or that guy needed a life). Looking at the work done for Mission: Impossible, I am 100% certain that nobody at Nintendo Power had ever beaten the game because they did a rubbish layout of the stages without going much into details. The whole coverage was entirely devoid of any strategy. For the game, I had already written a good review of it that included way, way more helpful tips in trying to beat it than they could do in those wasted pages. Looking at the letters of Mailbox, at least half of them had kids crying and begging to become a "counselor" while majority of them was bragging about their NES achievements. Hmm...so tell me...how many hours a day did these kids waste playing NES games (believe me, they take a very, very long time to master), and how much of that should have been put into homework from school? I love the endless pumping by the writers of the magazine, making sure people were directed to buy whatever the writers were pushing such as call line, cleaning products, technical repair, strategy guides, SNES, Game Boy, etc. Gosh, that Nester character was so annoying that he needed to be entirely cut out of the magazine. The comics were so boring, and they became page wasters. In the later issues, the layout of maps had improved considerably, and that alone will make Nintendo Power worth looking at. However, it was still limited to certain games. Rarely ever the writers will choose the most challenging NES games for greater coverage probably because their "counselors" probably couldn't advance further in the levels, let alone beating them. Hence, lots of cheat codes were provided in every issue. The biggest mistake of the magazine was the combination of NES and Game Boy, and then eventually SNES, coverages into one magazine. The result? Less and less exposure, then eventually extinction, for NES games which is a shame since we all know that the NES console is arguably the greatest console ever in Video Game history. Now I think of it: that was probably the biggest reason why I stopped bothering with Nintendo Power. The simple solution? They should have had separate magazines for each console. Thus, a lot of games could have been given more exposure, say 15 to 20 NES games per issue with 5 to 10 pages per. Also, get rid of the irrelevant art work and boring details about something else that are totally unrelated with NES games. Stick with the maps, strategies, and walkthroughs. Then, the writers would have been sitting on a goldmine. They never bothered with NES games made before Nintendo Power first came out which is another shame. All in all, Nintendo Power is truly a joke of a magazine, and we've discovered how thoroughly excellent gamefaqs and youtube of the games are today.
top_profane.title: Reviewing Issues 1 to 30, What a Terrible Magazine.

top_profane.text:
This magazine is nothing more that politically correct garbage. I am reviewing the June 2014 issue, which is now in my trash can. That's right, NOT the recycle bin as highlights suggests in the front of the magazine. It's in my trash can.

First of all, I have nothing against seeing different races than my own in children's literature. But this magazine seems to accomplish it in such a way that it seems forced and not at all done in a way that just flows naturally. It became a game to me while reading it. "And what will our next nationality be?? Turn the page to find out!" Poem 1, white family. Story 1, Tex and Indi (no American white sounding Dick and Jane here-and I say this not because I WANT Dick and Jane but because it sounds like they went out of their way to pick names that DONT sound too American) multiple races portrayed. Story 2, Spanish, including a boy named Pablo, and many sentences in Spanish. Now, I have nothing against Spanish, but guess what? This is America! We speak English. I don't appreciate a magazine aiming language integration at my preschool age and younger children. Poem 2, white child. That's Silly page, every ethnicity you can possibly imagine represented. Story 3, African American family. Exercise action rhyme page, African American child. Activity page 2, Spanish father and son. The rest of the magazine is various animals. Now when you add this all up, you can see that High Five magazine has gone out of their way to make sure there is equal representation of all races, with extra consideration to making sure there are less whites than anything else. And that is where I get the "forced" feeling. It's like it's not okay to be white any more, we should be ashamed. I read many, many books and stories to my five children and I read about all nationalities and teach them about lots of cultures. But while doing that, I don't put down their own culture. This is what I felt when reading high five.

Second problem: "school" obsession. Seeing as how this magazine is aimed at young children under five, there are an awful lot of references to "school". Well at this age, it's not school, it's daycare. I happen to homeschool my children. I don't mind reading them stories about school, but this magazine goes overboard. The Tex and Indi story? School. Poem about fish? School (no pun intended). Picture of far and near? School bus right in center. The Summer Box story? School. That's a large portion of the magazine! American children have a lot more in their lives besides just school! What about sibling relationships? Travel? Holidays? Learning new things? Birthdays! Pets! Seasons! Learning about animals? I can think of a million things my kids would be excited to read a story about! This magazine is LAME when it comes to a variety of stories. It's like they sat around saying "let's teach them about Spanish and school!"..I'm surprised there isn't a full page spread on the new food groups.

Lastly, my two youngest children didn't even enjoy the magazine. They couldn't relate to most of it! We don't speak Spanish, we don't go to school (they are 1 and 3!). They love to read, and I mean LOVE it. We read on average 5-10 books every single day. When they don't like something, that speaks volumes to me.

Another warning. In small print inside the first page you will find the following: sometimes we make our list of customer names available to other carefully screened companies whose products might be of interest to you. TRANSLATION....we sell your personal information. Something to think about!

I used to love highlights magazine as a child. This magazine is trash. I would never subscribe to it. I hope readers are able to see my review for what it is, just an honest review of what I saw, and not make this in to a debate about your beliefs versus mine. I'm not leaving this review for any reason other than to point out to parents what this magazine is like, in my opinion.
top_profane.title: Politically correct trash

top_profane.text:
I've been with the Fortean Times since way back at issue 31. Over the years since then it has got more glossy, but it still never ceases to amaze me. This latest issue is no exception.
Fortean Times is a monthly mag founded in 1973 to continue the work of Charles Fort, who, in the early part of the 20th Century was among the first to speculate that mysterious lights in the sky might be from outer space; coiner of the term "teleportation", and chronicler of all that these days are considered the realm of "The X Files". The magazine continues his tradition of reporting "damned" phenomena in an objective manner.
The "damned" covers a wide range, and in the pages of this magazine you'll find articles on such varied subjects as religious miracles, the latest happenings in UFOlogy, sightings of strange or out-of-place animals, and the weirdest cartoons in Christendom. Regular favourites include the "Strange deaths" column which details the many varied ways the human race can find to shuffle off this mortal coil.
This issue is well up to usual standards.
First up in the news section there is a report on how the Queen Mother's death led to a holiday park fire when the curse of the Koh-I-Noor diamond struck again; a reported 120 million year old map found carved in stone in the Ural mountains; a report of a family of nine children, all given up for adoption, who became close friends without knowing of their relationship and some great snippets on current advances in astronomy - including the interesting news that out towards the stars Pioneers 10 and 11 are slowing down. Not by much, but nobody knows why!
There's more news on an American on trial for murder who believed he was back in time killing Adolf Hitler; news of man-made structures off the coasts of Malta and the Isle of Wight; the latest on the Roberto Calvi Itlaian masonic banking affair; how to fold a dollar bill to see a representation of the Twin Towers burning; the origins of the stories about "The Curse of the Mummy" and the latest from the world of cryptozoology, including news on the search for a hairy man-beast in North East India.
And all that is before we get to the main articles, the meat of the magazine.
The first of the feature articles is a long piece on David Berkowitz, the "Son of Sam" murderer, who the writer Jon Vincent Sanders believes was not working alone, and was a member of a black magic underground in New York. I wasn't quite convinced by the article - it covered the history well, but didn't provide any strong evidence for the writer's theory.
The second article, by FT editor Paul Sieveking, is the sort of thing the magazine does so well. It is a run down of the history of feral children - those brought up by cats, dogs or monkeys. The writer traces many stories from all ages of human history, summarises the main points succinctly, and provides an exhaustive list of references. This is the way research on so-called "Weird" topics should be done.
The main article in this issue is a long profile of Sci-Fi legend Philip K Dick, detailing both his place in Fortean literature, and also his influence on current Sci-Fi cinema, particularly with reference to the latest blockbuster "Minority Report". Impressively, FT has garnered opinions on Dick from most of the big names in Brittish genre fiction, and some of them are very forthright in their views. There's also an investigation into Dick's very own "damned" life, including the super-intelligence VALIS that he believed he was in communication with.
The rest of the magazine is taken up by a couple of short articles, including a look at some of the odder events of the Queen mother's life, an extensive letters section that's, as usual, entertaining and enjoyable, and a reviews section covering books on George Bush's connection to big business, Jack the Ripper, and seeing ghosts; a review of "Minority Report" and more of the brilliant cartoons that are studded through the mag.
I write genre fiction, and this magazine is a constant source of ideas for stories, but above all else, it's the entertainment that counts, and FT provides that, consistently.

top_profane.text:
I subscribed to City Journal hoping to find another intelligent and courageous publication with a right slant, of some sort, be it libertarian, paleoconservative, or just a journal of common sense. Such publications are dwindling. City Journal does not describe itself as any of those things, but its reputation is that of a right leaning advocate of economic and individual freedom. I just received my first issue-Winter 2013. Disappointing.

Let's start with E.D. Hirsch's "Vocabulary: The Key to Upward Mobility". Some solid ideas, backed by bad reasoning with little human insight applied. Hirsch's ideas for overhauling the curriculum are fine--domain immersion for example. What's bothersome is Hirsch's Apserger's-like method of cherry picking study after study, citing correlations, making huge assumptions and never questioning the question in the first place: What is education and do we all need it? He starts with the very tired statistic that college grads make more money, tells us that SAT scores are a good predictor of graduation rates and income, that vocabulary size and "life chances" are related, and other such bare facts. True, as facts, but even a smart 6th grader could raise legitimate questions here. Once we ask those questions and see the falsehoods mere statistics generate, we can ask real questions. His heart MIGHT be in the right place, but his efforts go nowhere.

John Buntin's "The LAPD Remade" praises the work of new Commissioner Bill Bratton. The piece focuses on police-community relations, especially the Black community, said to be made better by Bratton's personal touch. But Buntin paints an overly bright picture here. He cites stats that show a decline in LA crime. First, there have been many accusations of falsification of crime statistics in LA. The same thing happened here in Philadelphia under Mayor Rendell, who never stopped patting himself on the back on the basis of those lies. Second, again, mere stats won't due. In a state of war, there are periodic states of peace, before the next storm, as when one group pushes the other out. To assess LA's crime, one needs to be down at the street level, to see the root causes. Citing official stats or getting quotes from government official on how good a job they are doing doesn't cut it.

If Buntin and Hirsch are well intentioned but off target, what of Paul Starobin's "Irrepressible Moscow"? Starobin, a former writer for the leftist New Republic and National Geographic, writes a piece that has both the fluffiness and dishonesty of a typical NG article. Beyond dishonesty even, is his mention of the "suppression of civil liberties" in Moscow because of the arrest of Pussy Riot! Starobin claims they were arrested for "chanting a novel prayer". This is such a distortion of the truth it should be upgraded to an OUTRIGHT LIE. There have been many Russian patriots jailed under Putin's leadership. Pussy Riot is not among them. It gets worse still, as Starobin, in his best attempt to downplay the demographic and social crisis caused by immigration, first forgets to tell us that these Caucasian immigrants are criminals and gangsters, protected by the corrupt Putin regime who make daily life for many Russians intolerable. Starobin goes on to cheerily tell us that these immigrants soon assimilate. No sign of that yet.

Another New Republic product, Adam Kirsch profiles Jewish leftist Peruvian writer and politician Mario Vargas Llosa. Kirsch's article has a sympathetic tone and we can see why as the former socialist and advocate of free trade and "liberty", is certainly no former leftist. Ilosa's idea of liberty is abortion, euthanasia, open borders, unrestricted immigration, and an end to all nationalisms. The common ground of rootless economic libertarians and rootless neo Marxists is laid bare. No wonder the Peruvians rejected him. Better a corrupt old style strongman than a leftist ideologue intent on destruction of a people.

But its time for some comedy: "The GOP and the City" by Edward L. Glaeser. Oh why do the big cities reject the GOP, asks Glaeser, when the Republicans have such good ideas? The question alone makes me chuckle. Cited as a good idea is No Child Left Behind! Now I'm rolling on the floor. If only the Republicans would get their message out, the urban dwellers might vote for them. Forget for a second whether the Republicans have good ideas--I think they do talk a good game--and forget whether they really back up their words once in power---they rarely do--but Glaeser is so clueless that he doesn't realize that the voters in big cities have heard the message. They rejected it Edward and continue to do so. Does Glaeser ever get down to street level and meet people? Does he know the cities at all. Again, a problem presents itself and City Journal finds itself without the insight to understand the issue.

And then there is Pierre Manent, whose incoherent attempt to trace the origins of the modern Nation-State may have caused me to cancel my subscription. He may have read Cicero but I can't see he understood his mind, method or intentions. His statement that the Middle Ages "...doesn't lend itself to our comprehension because it had no experience of an adequately coherent human order.", is a laughable product of narrow, ideologically oriented mind. I question if he even read Machiavelli beyond The Prince, and he completely ignores the real political, military and geographic factors that led to the rise of the state in the 17th century. Ideas do matter, but Manent got the wrong ideas. He actually is brighter than I give him credit for here, but in the end he is another neo-conservative. I charge clients $90. an hour for my work. I spent 30 minutes on this article. Since you can't refund time itself, I request that the publisher of City Journal refund me $45. for my time.

It isn't all bad. Like good social scientists, the writers at City Journal can turn out some good articles about economic policy and this issue has a view on the boom in Texas, the growth of Washington DC and the pension problem in California. Also, Heather MacDonald and Theodore Dalrymple are two bright spots. Dalrymple I am familiar with and always enjoy his writings. MacDonald I knew less about but I had come across some of her writings and was happy to read her here.

But the good is outnumbered at City Journal. Still, it is more palatable than the faux conservative American Conservative magazine, and not as left Republican as the once right wing National Review. I'll stick with the Rockford Institute's Chronicles: A Magazine of American Culture and Quarterly Review from the UK.

My analysis of City Journal ? A soft left establishment Republican ( ie neoconservative) publication that is fighting a lost cause with a losing stategy. Defensive, timid, shallow, sycophantic, positivist, City Journal is using the same tone, the same strategies and positions that led to the total defeat of the conservative movement. At best it is a losing rear guard action in a war already lost. In some cases, this may be intentional, as the neo cons intentionally destroyed the conservative movement. In others it is just naiveté in the extreme. CJ lacks soul. It lacks depth of thinking so it comes up empty. The perfect hand picked opponent for the left establishment.

I've subscribed to Golf Digest (GD) for most of the past two decades but stopped paying for it a few years back; I've been getting a year's subscription as an included freebie with entrance to my annual local golf expo. However, I've been skimming and skipping more and reading less of the magazine each year for the past half dozen years and it has deteriorated so much that I'd never bother spending a penny on it these days - it has become inundated with a strange blend of tabloid journalism and snobbish pseudo-sophistication that has tremendously diluted any useful or interesting content about the sport of golf.

Think of some of the worst things about the sport of golf. Over the past five or so years, Golf Digest has actually been PROMOTING many of those negative aspects.

Let's take the male-dominated chauvinism of the sport, as the primary example. A lot of women are turned off by what they see as a sport primarily played by men who primarily are happy with the status quo of few women participants in the sport. Anyone who doubts that this is still the case should go to a typical golf course on a weekend - one will see at least a 5-to-1 ratio of male to female golfers, maybe even a much higher ratio - there have been plenty of times I've played here in the upper Midwest on a Saturday or Sunday when the only female on the course was the beer cart girl. What has GD done to appeal to women golfers, to address the sport of golf from a women's perspective? Other than have a cover photo in a recent issue of a shirtless Rory McIlroy posing as a Greek god, practically nothing.

Some years back, Golf Digest made available to subscribers a special women's golf insert to the magazine that could be included in one's issues if the subscriber requested it. I requested it for my wife and daughter and never received a single copy with that women's golf insert nor do I recall hearing anything further about it. Perhaps the publisher decided to cancel this option almost instantly figuring that if few people responded after maybe only a one-time notice of the option, that it wasn't of interest to anyone, rather than give reasonable notice and then see how many people wanted to take advantage of the optional content.

I haven't sat down with several years worth of GD issues and done a specific count, but I would guess that at most, half of the issues even mention the LPGA Tour or have any content on an LPGA golfer, and if there is such content, it is generally limited to maybe two pages; in contrast, every issue features interviews or articles about at least two PGA golfers and tips and pointers from three to four more. Several times a year the instructional pages will contain a tip from an LPGA touring pro or female club pro. But that's it for content addressed to women. My elementary school-aged daughter loves golf and I've tried to get her to read Golf Digest whenever I've received a new copy, hoping it will keep her interest in the sport high. A few times she's sat down, flipped through the magazine, and said "Dad, this is just about men golfers." I'll point out an interview with an LPGA golfer or instructional tip from a female teaching pro (generally never both in the same issue, though) and her response was "That's two pages out of ... what, maybe 150 pages? This magazine is for boys." You know what? She's 100% correct.

Golf Digest's decision essentially to ignore women golfers and include the barest modicum of female content necessary to avoid a label of completely male-centered, is really short-sighted. What sort of person subscribes to a golf magazine? That's right, golfers and fans of professional golf. The most fertile source of new subscribers to an activity-based magazine is those who are new to the activity - the magazine will certainly increase its subscriber base if it promotes the activity. The largest untapped source of new golfers and thus new golf magazine subscribers is women, yet Golf Digest virtually ignores the entire gender, and avoids a chance to grow both the sport and their subscriber base [* FN 1] And for that matter, once the initial Tiger Woods decade subsided, GD has virtually ignored minorities in the sport of golf.

Sadly, the most prominence Golf Digest has given to women has been in their annual skin.. err... fitness issue, where for the past three years they have featured a scantily clad and attractive female on the cover who is supposedly promoting greater "fitness" among golfers. Two year ago it was arrogant, snappy, and plastic surgery-enhanced former Golf Channel personality Holly Sonders and last year it was model Paulina Gretzky whose tenuous connection to golf is that she is the fiancée of PGA golfer Dustin Johnson. Rather pathetically, unless a woman shoots a 58 in a tournament or wins a calendar-year grand slam - two or three LPGA majors in one year aren't necessarily enough, ask outrageously talented but less-than svelte Inbee Park how much attention she received from GD after winning not one or two but THREE LPGA majors in 2013 - it's not likely she will be featured in her sport's so-called premier magazine. A woman has a better chance of appearing in Golf Digest or on the magazine's website if she's a pretty WAG (wives and girlfriends)of a PGA Tour player than a remarkably skilled LPGA Tour player. Hell, I bet Amanda Dufner, the very pretty soon-to-be ex-wife of frumpy PGA Championship winner Jason Dufner, has received as much coverage in the magazine and in the magazine's website as any current or future LPGA hall of fame player over the past half decade.

Amid outcry that the magazine is objectifying women and ignoring actual female athletes, this year the "fitness" issue features 20 year-old American star Lexi Thompson, a four-time LPGA Tour winner including a major last summer, as its cover model. Problem addressed, right? Golf Digest would have to be freakin' obtuse to screw up that choice of a cover model, wouldn't they? Well, indeed, the publishers at Golf Digest are just that dumb. They had Lexi posed topless. OK, she does have a small towel draped over her shoulders that covers the center of her breasts, but otherwise, she's naked from the waist up. My family and I are big Lexi Thompson fans - three summers ago, we attended an LPGA major and followed her for the whole 18 holes. How the hell am I supposed to show my daughter a major story on her favorite golfer when Golf Digest chose to show her nearly nude in a cover photo meant to appeal to randy male readers??

I honestly think that the publishing crew and senior editors at Golf Digest either came over straight from Maxim magazine or are a bunch of middle-aged dufuses (or is the plural of dufus spelled "dufi"?) who desperately want to be hip-and-trendy and think their subscription woes are caused not by people going to digital sources rather than hard copy magazines, but by the lack of "racy" content. The annual skin/fitness covers drive away women readers and who do they attract? For every guy at a train station or airport news kiosk who is looking for something to read on his commute who might grab the "fitness" issue, at least another one will buy Sports Illustrated instead, not wanting to be seen reading a "skin rag" in public. It's pretty well known among males of puberty age or older that the internet is overflowing with graphic imagery of a purile nature, so I doubt a single guy has decided to subscribe to GD thinking "I've been looking for a source for photos of scantily-clad females and until now I've not been able to find one, I've always thought that golf was a fuddy-duddy sport, but this cover story has changed my mind! Ring me up as a new Golf Digest subscriber!"

Online right now on GD's website there is an instructional article featuring a tip from Blair O'Neal, one of the more famous women in the world of golf, and certainly not among the most skilled. Hmm, Blair O'Neal, an LPGA star you are asking? Nope. She's got no bio on the LPGA site (and they retain bios of pretty much anyone who played a regular season in the past 15 to 20 years or so - the head pro of one local course played on the LPGA Tour for a few years back in the 90's earning not much more than enough to cover her travel expenses and she's still listed on the LPGA website) nor does she have a Wikipedia page, which nearly all athletes of any even modest level of accomplishment have. So who the heck is she? O'Neal was a contestant on several seasons of the Golf Channel's reality golf competition show The Big Break who is best known not for winning anything (I spent but a minute looking but found nothing to suggest she's made the cut of a single LPGA event if she even played in any, likely on sponsors exemptions because of her semi-celebrity status). Blair O'Neal is known for being cute, blond, leggy, and posing in a bikini in the opening credits introducing the season's Big Break contestants. We have several hundred present and past female golfers who actually have accomplished something by earning and keeping an LPGA Tour card, yet not one of those athletes, who, unlike Blair O'Neal, actually are world-class golfers who have accomplished something in the sport of golf, were deemed by Golf Digest to be worthy of consultation on golf instruction. Yep, for Golf Digest, easy on the eyes in a short skirt is more important than actual knowledge and expertise.

I did just recall one article in the magazine in which they sent a cute female staffer who owned at least one tight tee-shirt undercover to work as a beer cart girl and surprisingly, did report rather candidly that the typical sort of guy such female golf course employees encounter (the pompous, windbag sort of Golf Guy who ironically is the target audience of Golf Digest) is quite a tool. But that hardly is an article of interest to women who like the sport of golf and quite frankly I think the main reason it got published was some boss at Golf Digest took a look at his intern or staff member's chest and said to himself "We've got to think of some way to get that published in this magazine!"

So not only does Golf Digest essentially ignore, if not offend, women golfers, but they also ignore families who like to play golf. I think I can recall a single article in all the years I've read and received Golf Digest that addresses how to further one's children's interest in the sport (and the more I think of it, the more I believe I could very well be mistaken and it was actually an article about kids in Tennis magazine that is coming to mind). Multiple issues a year address "golf buddy" trips to Myrtle Beach the Upper Peninsula, etc..., but in the history of the magazine, not a single article has ever appeared on destinations for a family golf trip with children nor have they ever done a single article or list of teaching pros who specialize in teaching kids or done any instructional articles aimed at kids.

The publishers of Golf Digest are not targeting the typical golfer, they are targeting "Golf Guy," a male golfer possessing multiple negative aspects in various subfields of arrogance and snobbery. There are a multitude of Golf Guys on any online golf forum, they all add about 20-70 yards onto the length of their average drives as they boast about their games, they all claim to be low and mid-handicap players and claim that any person who isn't physically and mentally capable of breaking 90 regularly or who loses more than a ball or two per round is a pathetic hack who should stick to the range and not clutter-up and slow down the course for "real" golfers (forgetting that fewer than 10% of all golfers shoot in the 80s or lower). Golf Guy arrogantly dismisses complaints about the cost of the sport, claiming a $75 greens fee is a "bargain." A typical Golf Guy will claim to finish a round easily in two hours or less (virtually impossible without rushing frantically, on a wide-open course and without possessing near-scratch playing ability), he claims he plays at least 3 to 4 rounds per week and will make disparaging comments about the lack of time-management skills of anyone who states they cannot play as frequently. However, unless Golf Guy is entertaining clients on the course regularly or is taking his kids to the course with him, he's not a parent or isn't much of one at all, neglecting his family for his hobby if he is playing that often, given how time-intensive the game of golf generally is.

If you've played enough golf, you've seen plenty of Golf Guys on the course - they're the ones wearing wool dress pants on a 95*F day because "they respect the traditions of the game," they pseudo-respectfully refer to famous golfers and treat them as gods (even long-dead or more populist ones or ones who were arrogant horse's arses [** FN2]) as "Mister Hogan" or "Mister Palmer" or "Mr. Nicklaus." The typical Golf Guy was the first one wearing dorky white belts when that silly 70's style revived, and he wouldn't be caught dead in less than a $150 Bobby Jones golf shirt with anything less than the latest model of $500 driver, $300 putter and $1,000 set of irons, all top-tier brands, in his bag. I once got paired up with a Golf Guy when I walked-on to a golf course and I asked him what he was doing when I saw him snooping into my golf bag. He responded "I like to know what sort of equipment a man has before I play him for money." I responded "I don't gamble and if I did, why would you think I'd play someone like you for money anyway?" What a *(&%@&()@! I am positive he's been enjoying Golf Digest a whole lot these past several years.

Golf Digest's publishers have decided to cater to Golf Guy's snobbish whims and fancies in the past few years, so the magazine regularly features reviews of $70,000 to $100,000 luxury cars (this is related to golf because ... because apparently nothing less will hold two sets of golf clubs in the trunk nor be able to navigate to any local golf course ??), they have had multiple featured style articles on watches priced from $3,000 to $20,000 (because nothing less will enable a "real" golfer to get to his tee time promptly? Yet aren't most of these same guys claiming that no "real" golfer could hit a quality shot with the distraction of anything appearing on his wrist?), and they have other "golf style" articles that feature expensive, pretentious clothing, shoes, even cigars. To make Golf Guy feel like he's a really thoughtful, conscientious type of guy, GD includes a monthly column "Golf Saved Me!" with some sappy, feel-good, do-gooder tripe of a human interest story relating vaguely to golf.

Golf Digest does publish a lot of golf tips and instructional columns and articles including a monthly one by Tom Watson. Over the years I've read hundreds of these golf tips and I've come to realize a few things as I learn more about the game of golf. First, there are not one or two ways to skin the proverbial golf cat, there are many. There are probably a dozen or two or three different ways to impart moderate amounts of desirable left-to-right or right-to-left spin to hit a draw or fade and there are at least an equal number of ways to reduce excess spin to avoid the dreaded hooking and slicing. Second, I don't think many so-called golf instructional experts understand golf as well as they pretend and third, I don't think most of those golf instructors are as clear of communicators as they think they are. Out of those hundreds of golf tips, I can recall and remember about five that have actually helped: aim right side of fairway but swing parallel to dead center to hit a draw; stand with feet closer than shoulder length for pitches; scoop out a dollar bill-sized divot hitting sand first for bunker shots; take 2 clubs more, position ball back by right foot, take 3/4 swing for knock-down shots into the wind; and after calculating break on the green, add at least 1/3 more, as amateurs chronically underestimate break. Out of hundreds of tips, I should have been able to retain more knowledge and to the extent that my learning style for sports is not to blame, I attribute this failing to a) often mediocre, less-than-completely-clear writing in instructional articles; and b) lack of editorial consistency - some of these various tips on how to cure a slice, for instance, are simply not consistent with each other. While golf is probably the most complex of all sports to understand and master, I have retained and utilized a significantly greater portion of the instructional articles I've read in Tennis and Ski magazine, for instance and have found them more clearly written and useful, than those that appear in Golf Digest.

Golf Digest's equipment "reviews" have fluctuated in content but are of minimal worth. At times they are of the "Here's some marketing synopses on the latest drivers, iron sets, putters and balls coming out this year" and during other periods they have supposed play-test reviews; I'll confess I cannot recall from the most recent equipment issue if they are presently doing just the "Here's what our advertisers tell us to print" or if they are in the phase where they do alleged reviews of the equipment. Either way, the substantial majority of equipment they review/highlight is made by their largest advertisers and other than golf balls, it is always high-end equipment. Despite the fact that the majority of people getting into the game of golf start out with some sort of modest-priced beginner's all-in-one set, they have never, ever done a comparison of say the typical $150 to $300 sets by Wilson, Dunlop, Top Flite, Adams, etc... nor compared them to higher end clubs (i.e. they are doing the car magazine equivalent of reviewing only Porches, BMWs, Cadillacs, and Ferraris, without a single review of an Accord, Camry or even a Civic or Escort). Nor has GD ever done even a single article on how to select used equipment, particularly an used set for a beginner (often a better option because the 1st and 2nd tier equipment actually does hit straighter and longer than cheap beginner sets). Maybe if several of the big online auction sellers of used golf clubs took out full page ads in GD this would change.

I have a problem with Golf Digest ball tests because they appear to be based solely on snobbishness and reputation of the ball manufacturer - in fact, the ball reviews are called the "Hot List: Golf Balls" - what matters is what is "hot" - i.e. trendy, not necessarily what works the best. Accordingly to Golf Digest, if it's small, round and has a "Titleist" logo on it, it's an automatic inclusion into the Hot List! as a best-of-the-best product in every price range, something that does not correlate to my experiences at all playing with that brand of balls. Let me admit that there is an excellent reason why more touring pros play Titleist Pro-V1 and Pro-V1x than all other balls combined - from my experience and that of every other good golfer (well, I'm too inconsistent to be called "good" but I do make a lot of good shots) I know, Pro-V1s do go the longest and have the best feel and spin on the greens of any ball on the market - they really are the best. However, I have found that all of Titleist's other, cheaper balls are mediocre performers against other similarly-priced balls, yet invariably, without exception, ever, GD rates all Titleist balls, not just the deserving Pro-V1s, as 5/5-star/gold-rated products.

I also don't care for Golf Digest's social tone, beyond just the snobbery about non-golf accessories and luxury items. This month's cover story is about long-hitting (both of the ball and allegedly the drug pipe) PGA star Dustin Johnson who recently came off a supposed self-imposed hiatus from the game while he "addressed some aspects of his personal life," which he has not specified but apparently has not denied the generally-accepted statements that he was in treatment for polysubstance abuse. There is also a fairly credible bit of information floating around that Johnson has for years had a cocaine problem, he failed a tour drug test, it was not his first time failing such a test, and his break from playing was actually a secret PGA Tour suspension. There are also supposedly reputable rumors that Johnson had been shagging the wife of one of his fellow Tour members, ending their marriage; gee, Tiger-haters, how do you reconcile your love of Johnson with your hatred of Tiger in light of their at minimum equivalent marriage-wrecking adultery? While I am glad that Johnson has straightened out his life to the point that he's playing well again, I don't think he deserves a cover story in Golf Digest nor the sympathy he's receiving from Golf Guy and Golf Digest unless he comes clean, admits his addictions, and starts to doing anti-drug promotions. Most iterations of Golf Guy are probably willing to forgive and hero-worship Dustin Johnson but loathe and will never forgive Tiger Woods, who longtime contributing GD writer Dan Jenkins recently lampooned quite obnoxiously in the magazine for his indiscretions [*** FN3], which I believe represents the sentiments of the magazine hiding behind a curmudgeonly journalistic legend. You see, Johnson is engaged to a blond beauty and committed the less-than-fully-pleasant-and-not-quite-socially-acceptable act of buying illegal substances sold by criminal syndicates who murder thousands of innocent people each year, whereas Woods committed the much more heinous crime of, first, being a person of color marrying a beautiful, naturally blond woman (even worse than if she was an unnatural blond) and second, cheating on her multiple times, most atrociously with women who were for the most part less attractive than his naturally blond, blue-eyed wife. While Tiger Woods is the biggest star the sport has probably ever known, I am confident that there are plenty of other PGA stars whose marriages ended because a cocky athlete always on the road was unfaithful, yet because those other stars were white, Golf Digest took no notice of them.

The magazine is not altogether worthless, though, because of the pro-tour golfer content. I do read many of the interviews of PGA (and the rare LPGA) star, which tend to be pretty well-written. I like the "What's In My Bag" monthly column where they show a photo of a PGA or very occasionally an LPGA touring pro's bag and clubs and we get the details and story behind why he is playing which equipment and what little superstitious item he carries for good luck. And there's a couple of semi-regular articles written by one or more anonymous journeyman PGA pros that supposedly candidly say what is right, wrong, and/or interesting about playing on the PGA Tour that a non-touring pro might not have realized. Finally, the magazine will usually include a pretty detailed and sometimes interesting article on whichever course is on this year's rotation for the U.S. Open, British Open (no, Golf Guy, it's not "THE Open" any more than UVA is "THE University" or OSU is "THE Ohio State University") or PGA Championship and that provides a nice background if I've got the time to sit down that weekend and watch some golf.

In conclusion, this magazine was never perfect but it has really deteriorated over the past half decade. The magazine essentially ignores women, completely ignores integrating kids into the sport, and increasingly has focused on a whole lot of content that really has nothing to do with the sport of golf from either the perspective of an amateur playing the sport or a fan of the professional tours. Golf Digest caters to the sort of guy who boasts about his healthy lifestyle and respect for family values, yet likes to sneak off to smoke cigars in the nudie bars, who boasts how generous he is as he tips the cute-but-inattentive waitress 30% while leaving barely 5% for the outstanding but plain and overweight woman who waits his table the next night. If the phrase "arrogant, snobby putz" can accurately be applied to you and you like golf, well, Golf Digest will be just your cup of tea. It's been a couple of years since I last subscribed to Golf magazine, but I remember it equaling in most ways Golf Digest's strengths or mediocrities and not having the issues and weaknesses such as promoting male chauvinism or snobbery like Golf Digest so blatantly does, so I'd recommend Golf over Golf Digest.

FOOTNOTES:

[* FN1] However, for any honest person wanting to talk about gender inequality in sports, one of the several fingers that most deserve to be pointed must be pointed squarely at women themselves. If it's not figure skating, gymnastics or diving, most (but not all) women, if given control of the remote, won't watch sports on television, not men's sports, not women's sports, not any kind of sports. When I attended an LPGA major a few years back, not counting families and heterosexual couples, groups of male fans there outnumbered female fans by a 2 to 1 margin, at least. I've also attended quite a few Div. I women's basketball, volleyball and hockey games over the years and there are always more male fans in the seats than female fans. Next time you watch a WTA match on television, you'll see more men in the stands than women and Anna Kournikova retired years ago.

[** FN2] Golf Guy's reverence, even hero-worship, of a number of golf legends not just as athletes but as people in general makes very little sense. Ben Hogan was by any reasonable person's standards an aloof, unpleasant person who routinely gave snarky non-answers to questions from journalists or fellow golf pros alike and screwed over a friend of his, who lost a position as a golf pro at a prestigious country club because Hogan failed to send in letter of recommendation. Just last week while sitting in a guest role commenting on the Player's Championship, John Feinstein (who might possess more current knowledge about golf and the PGA tour than any journalist alive), knowingly referred to Jack Nicklaus and Tom Watson as "not nice people."

[*** FN3] For many years Golf Digest featured a commentary column by covering-the-PGA-since-the-dawn-of-time sports journalist Dan Jenkins, who for reasons that are unknown to me, is lauded and revered by probably 99.9% of the Golf Guy community. Jenkins was friends with Hogan and knew all of the legends of golf during the middle of the 20th century and his specialty, aside from his access to all of these golf stars and legends, was his allegedly brilliant wit. To me, Jenkins was to sports humor what Carrottop is to regular humor or graphic stories of deer hunting would be to romance on a first date with a PETA member. He's the sort of crusty, pompous old goat who might call someone an idiot for claiming cigarettes are unhealthy or declare someone is "un-American" for stating that the legislature's attempt to pass laws promoting religion in schools violates the First Amendment (these are just hypotheticals, not actual examples that specifically and accurately can be attributed to Jenkins, but I wouldn't be even slightly surprised if they actually were applicable). For quite a while Jenkins seems to have taken a sabbatical and/or retired, but a few months back he published in Golf Digest a snarky, pseudo-clever "interview" with Tiger Woods, who will not in fact talk with or grant Jenkins a real-life interview because of some past comment or another. Woods got all upset and by foolishly going online in protest, guaranteed that a washed-up, bitter old has-been got triple the readership his article otherwise would have received. Jenkins despises Tiger Woods and while I have no proof of it, I'd bet at least a pet, if not a future grandchild, that if Tiger was blond and blue-eyed, Jenkins wouldn't have much, if any, problem with him; it's awfully hard for an old leopard to change his spots, few white octogenarian Texans have always been open-minded about dark skinned peoples, and nothing that Tiger Woods has done should reasonably inspire that much contempt.
top_profane.title: Once Decent Magazine Has Degenerated Into Maxim (Golf Edition)

top_profane.text: Plugged-In is a welcome resource in our home.  We have used plugged-in online for years to obtain reviews on movies, music, games, tv shows, etc. Let's face it parents, we don't have time to do all the reviews ourselves.  I for one, am thankful there is a trusted source out there that states the facts; good, bad or indifferent. That's exactly what Plugged-in does.  They rate a movie, for instance and highlight the pro-social content, objectional content and end with a summery/advisory.  Of course, it's a magazine published by an Evangelical Ministry so there are articles relating the the world of Christianity.  If you're easily offended by the teachings of the Jesus Christ, or view points of Christians, go easy on the articles and stick to the reviews.  But don't blast the magazine for doing exactly what it set out to accomplish and reach the target it intended.  As parents, when we have questions regarding our kid's friends, we go to the source - those who know the friends.  Regardless of their faith, I want to know what they know about "Johnny"; the analogy is the same w/ entertainment.  After reading the magazine or going online, my husband and I, along w/ our children can make wise decisions regarding what goes in their heads in the name of entertainment.  Some may call that "legalism", we call it wise parenting.  You know the old cliche - garbage in, garbage out!  We wouldn't fill their bodies w/ garbage, why fill their precious minds w/ garbage?
top_profane.title: Must Have Resource

top_profane.text: This magazine was offered as a $5 deal and I was disappointed in the magazine itself.  I did not want to renew, but especially did not want to renew for the pricy renewal fee.  However - the magazine sent me a letter stating that I was going to be automatically renewed unless I contacted them!  Which was nearly impossible!!!  ESPN The Magazine is a scam.  I have called several times already trying to let them know I do not want this magazine anymore and cannot get through to anyone.  I am so disappointed with this.  It's bad enough that your magazine is terrible, but even worse that I have to go through all of this garbage just to cancel a crappy magazine.  Don't sign up!! Not even if they are promising a free issue!  What a pain.
top_profane.title: Horrible experience

top_profane.text: When people said ‘it’s so full of ads’ I shrugged it off, but holy crap. When did this magazine go so far downhill?! In the first 70 pages, 50 are ads. There’s still a few good recipes but overall, so much garbage, fluff, and ADS galore.
top_profane.title: They should call it Southern Advertisements

top_profane.text: Um. This is 100 of self-promotion garbage. There is almost no inspiration or valuable home improvement insights. It’s all about THEM and their crap. And why put yourself on every cover?!? You ain’t Oprah. Man, Sunset Magazine is a million times better. Unsubscribe. Literally.

top_profane.text: What happened to Women's Health magazine?? top_profane.text:
Ok so I'll start off by saying this magazine could be great, because of the talent they have signed. But after about 6 issues, and two I didn't receive, which I will touch on later, I realized this magazine was total bull. I have seen so called interview after interview in their magazine contradicted by real life interviews online.
The magazine also tries to push products that have zero effect on your body, but a dent in you wallet, such as no2. No2 is not real, you can't effect that by taking crap in a bottle,.sorry but I went to college, I know better.
The service is garbage. They didn't send me two issues and both times gave me extensions on the magazine, and after the second time that they refused to send me the issue, the Mr Olympia issue mind you, I canceled my subscription.
This is vague but the experience was just bad, while my other magazine comes through every month, and delivers, this one falls well short.
About me, I'm 6'4, 270lbs, been training for 5 years, at 23 I have 4 years college experience which is in multiple fields, including biochemistry. This magazine didn't make sense to me, a natural bodybuilder, who educated himself and have sacrificed so much, you want an education, stay away from this magazine and read some stuff from Dorian Yates, his teachings are great for naturals.
top_profane.title: such a waste of talent

top_profane.text:
Money magazine is better than all of the others simply because it provides pragmatic information in an easily digestible form. It offers financial advice that just makes sense for most people to at least consider. Over the years I have had subscriptions to many of the other financial periodicals like Fortune, Smart Money, Forbes, Kiplinger's, WSJ, FT, IBD, etc. and with the exception of Barron's I have found most of those to be highly unuseful and full of advice that just makes little sense to the average investor. Sure Money has problems keeping things fresh and they do recycle information frequently, but the editor does try to provide practical information so people can make reasonable decisions. Money has the same problem all financial periodicals do, namely they need to sell magazines. In order to do that they must react to the pop finance topics and offer some reasonably interesting articles to feed the herd. Can you imagine the reaction they would have encountered had they run monthly warnings of the housing bubble begging in 2002? Everyone I knew thought it would continue forever...fools, of course. Money was not on the front edge of the warning system. Certainly they had a part in creating and adding credibility to the real estate bubble and subsequent meltdown. But they do not stand alone; all publications were pumping the same bologna.

Money will occasionally remind its readers that index beating returns are highly improbable in the long run and stock picking as a primary means is not a good strategy for long term wealth building. They offer really nice "what if" and comparison strategies for analysis such as: lease vs. buy, buy vs. rent, or Ivy League vs. state college strategies. Sure they occasionally throw in some esoteric discussion on silly instruments like derivatives or ridiculous low yield bank savings accounts, but for the most part they tend to show reasonable responsibility and restraint by offering advice in the Graham and Dodd tradition.

Let's face it, personal finance is not that complicated. People who think it is are not thinking about it correctly. The tried and true methods aren't sexy and won't sell magazines if they printed the most effective methods for creating wealth every month. Money does a reasonable job providing confirmation of basic and effective investing strategies to the common investor in an entertaining way. However, I'd like to see them take a much more aggressive tone on topics like credit card debt, student loan debt, auto loan debt, etc. like Dave Ramsay does, only without all that religious baggage he loves to impose. It would also be nice if Money would rip apart and expose the scam investments like whole/variable life insurance policies, annuities, and the like. And ya, a little less of the fluffy Suzie Orman type codling would be nice.

I'd like to see Money dive a little deeper occasionally and expose real wealth killers. Here are a few thoughts: why not expose our tax system for the all consuming corrupt mess that it is and rally the masses to change it or at least remind people that it is not set up in their favor as a worker. It is strictly set up for business owners to get the most advantage. Or how about exposing all that old money held in trusts by family dynasties doing nothing to increase GDP and paying little in tax. How about analyzing the impact of the massive boomer generation retiring. Do you really think all of those seemingly ubiquitous "senior" discounts are going to exist for you in the future? Bahahahahah, think again. So many opportunities.

My personal favorite feature is the monthly money makeover. Usually some dopey married couple has pissed away their time and money on ridiculous things like a McMansion, boats, motorcycles, and his & hers BMWs then suddenly find themselves mid-life with little to no savings and a boat load of debt. I laugh at those morons. Money hires a financial adviser to break the bad news to them with a makeover plan that attempts gets them back on track. Good information can be garnered from these analyses.

I take issue with the lack of concern for inflation and ridiculous assumed returns on some investments. TVM seems to elude all financial writers and editors. I guess because it just isn't sexy and it involves a little grade school math. Few ever discuss the real rates of return and the actual real money people will need in retirement. Also, a major pet peeve of all of the periodicals is that they assume social programs will be available to retirees in fifteen plus years. They won't. I guarantee it. Social Security and Medicare are a fraud and should not be part of one's retirement planning.

Bottom line: Money is a good, solid financial publication that tries to offer the common investor reasonable information for making good decisions. Hard to find fault with that mission.
top_profane.title: Very Good Financial Periodical

top_profane.text:
Bust is an entertaining, highly readable magazine aimed at 20- and 30-something women who unashamedly self-identify as feminists. It reflects a heavy influence of NYC/Brooklyn and the indie scene. The journalism is not terribly political or hard-hitting, but I don't think it intends to be. Bust does occasionally discuss things like comprehensive sex ed, breastfeeding in public, and misogynistic commercial ads; however, for the most part, the content is on the lighthearted side. I have subscribed for almost a year, although I've read it intermittently off newsstands since 2005, and overall, I really enjoy reading it.

The great thing about Bust is that it has substance and is fun to read. There are never articles like "How to Look Thinner," "Choose the Right Anti-Aging Surgical Procedure," and other inane, self-hate-inducing garbage typically found in mainstream women's mags. By contrast, Bust has articles on female stand-up comedians, women who travel the world volunteering, and how to choose the right vibrator or electric guitar. (These are also great examples of how Bust has a more lighthearted tone than Ms. or [...].) [MM edit: Thanks a lot, Amazon, for editing out the word B**ch right there -- it's the name of a feminist magazine, not a derogatory term. Just shows you how far we still have to come.]

Bust's regular features include:

* Feminist interest columns -- Pop Tart, a tongue-in-cheek take on current pop culture; Museum of Femoribilia, with articles on women's cultural objects (swimsuit bra cups, restrictive petticoats, girls' toys, etc.) from the 1920s on and the feminist issues raised by them; and Mother Superior, by Ayun Halliday, who writes self-effacingly and hilariously about her kids' antics

* DIY projects -- Make your own queen-size headboard, reusable tote bags, mod-themed tea towels, pillbox hat, subversive cross-stitch art, etc.

* Recipes -- Chinese wontons, gingersnaps, tsimmes, mozzarella cheese (yes, how to curdle cheese), how to throw an eco-friendly Thanksgiving feast

* Boy du Jour -- A short interview with a hot, progressive, not-so-mainstream dude

* Fashion features -- Trend spotting; up-and-coming independent designers; a multi-page fashion spread illustrating a current trend (steampunk, outdoorsy hippie, cowgirl, 80s rock-chic)

* Interviews with 1-2 progressive celebrities

* Travel -- Articles on domestic and international destinations, with suggestions on restaurants, worthy hole-in-the-wall shops, things to do and see

* Sex -- Reader Q&A with Betty Dodson and, more recently, Carlin Ross (this can get graphic at times, but it's info we were all wondering anyway); Sex Files, a column discussing general women's health topics; the "One-Handed Read," stories similar to those in other popular women's mags (i.e., porn for women)

* Reviews of 1) not-so-mainstream beauty products, 2) recent indie music, 3) books written by or about women, and 4) recent indie movies produced/directed/written by or about women

* Comic strip detailing the trialz & tribz of a high school sophomore in the 1980s (by Esther Pearl Watson) and a feminist-themed crossword puzzle at the end -- I luuurve the comic strip and the crossword!

Bust is really big on indie musicians, especially those with a progressive bent, male or female. There is always at least one feature on a musician in each issue.

It's also important to note that Bust is not misandronistic in any way; in fact, they're very open about appreciating, crushing on, and having relations with men.

However, here's my one gripe: Bust has a somewhat "exclusive" vibe. Don't get me wrong -- they're not out there bashing people who don't subscribe to their indie ethos; they're just not inclusive of a wide audience. I agree with a previous reviewer about the general lack of inclusion of women of color (and I am white). There are, of course, some exceptions to this -- like the features on Eve, Rosario Dawson, Margaret Cho, Charlyne Yi, Sandrah Oh, and Rosie Perez, for example. But these features may be too few and far-between for women of color to feel truly championed. The bottom line is that Bust just aims for a very specific audience, which happens to be white, straight, crafty, indie/hipster feminists in their 20s and 30s. For some people, this may feel alienating. I occasionally feel like I'm not "hipster enough" for Bust, even though I'm pretty sure I fit into their intended demographic. ;)

Despite this, I continue to read Bust because I enjoy the positive media outlet it provides, especially compared to the likes of Glamour, Vogue, Cosmo -- which make ALL women feel like crap, regardless of heritage, size, and interests. Personally, I get enough positive value out of Bust as a whole to let the occasional self-doubt slide.

Overall, Bust is an enjoyable, lighthearted, pro-woman magazine. I'd suggest you check it out at Borders or B&N before subscribing, just to get an idea of its readability and look/feel. Recommended!
top_profane.title: Lighthearted & fun feminist mag
