top_rant.text:
I work full-time in law enforcement, having formerly worked as a university professor. I've been a subscriber to _S.W.A.T._ for over a year, and I also subscribe to a few other firearms- related magazines. Although I am evaluating _S.W.A.T._ in general, I'm using the January 2011 issue as a reference for my review because it's an excellent representative of a magazine which is remarkably consistent in quality and content. _S.W.A.T._ is a great periodical choice for members of law enforcement, especially those that work a beat, as it speaks directly to issues for the officers with boots on the street. At first, _S.W.A.T._ looks like all the other firearms publications on the rack, with boldly colored covers splashed with testosterone-fueled photos of a tactical ninja aiming a firearm at an off-camera bad guy and with enticing phrasing, such as "AR Superhero," "Don't Bet Your Life on the .380," and "An Army of One: Working without Backup." Yes, it looks like another who-is-more-macho gun rag, and it might need to in order to attract new readers, but, inside, _S.W.A.T._ outshines other publications, such as _American Cop_ or _Soldier of Fortune_.

First and foremost, the quality of writing is top-notch. In other firearms magazines I expect to be inundated with misspelled words, sentence fragments without reason, run-on sentences, nonsensical use of punctuation, intermittent subject-verb agreement, and tangents that go on purposelessly for paragraphs; this is my cardinal complaint about firearms publications, which are largely poorly written by what seems to be C-minus/D-plus English students. Anyone can expound on the subjects covered in this genre, but I am paying for professional writing ability. I challenge those who scoff at me on this point to find as poor writing even in frilly publications, such as _Us Weekly_ or _People_. _S.W.A.T._ is well-edited, well-written, structured with a Zen-like focus on the subject at hand, and I don't get a headache by tripping over poor writing structure and awful grammar skills. _S.W.A.T._ gets an A-plus in this regard.

Every page is full color, and this includes most advertisements, which occupy about half of the pages they appear on; however, not every page is splashed with ads, and _S.W.A.T. _`s evaluations of products do not acquiesce to its advertisers. If the product evaluated is junk, the article will honestly point this out. The January issue's "Don't Bet Your Life: Arguments against the .380" bellows that this pistol caliber is downright dangerous insurance, and I agree. This certainly demonstrates _S.W.A.T._'s integrity to be realistic in the face of advertisers' claims, and there are a slew of .380 pistol manufacturers that have to be shaking their collective fists at the magazine because of its candidness. You definitely will not find this type of honesty in other firearms magazines.

The editor's opening piece is interesting in that Denny Hansen doesn't just review the subjects listed in the table of contents. For example, in the January issue, he mentions the FBI's 2009 Uniform Crime Report, to advocate situational awareness, and a recall by Bushmaster for its ACR rifle, to increase buyers' awareness. I have not seen either item listed in any of the popular firearms magazines, yet.

"Mail Room" usually has four or five letters from readers, but this relatively small selection contains pertinent questions and commentary on prior issues, and each letter gets the editor's response. "Mail Room" does not waste space with favorable, eulogistic letters that only say, "Great mag! Keep up the good work!" much like will be found several times in the "Speak Out" section of _American Handgunner_. I really dislike when magazines will publish readers' letters but will not respond to the letters' questions in any meaningful way. Hansen not only repeats the essence of the question, but he commonly thanks the writer for their commentary, directly answers the question, isn't afraid to say that he doesn't know an answer or doesn't care for the writer's opinion, and promptly provides advice. In the January issue, one reader lionizes law enforcement officers as "murdering bastards" and gun grabbers, to which Hansen replies, in his usual, moderate, calm tone, that most in that profession are quite the opposite, with a closing but friendly barb of "[y]ou may want to take your hat off for awhile; I think you may be having an allergic reaction to tinfoil." This typifies the magazine's professionalism, confidence, and sense of humor, for the mean-spirited letter could not have been published in the first place.

There are always articles on training; tactical school evaluations; and old and new firearms, knives, and related products, such as scopes and holsters. The January issue includes articles on basics of escape-and-evasion techniques, training with white lights and lasers, training with .22 rifle trainers and conversions, a Defensive Edge armorer's course for AR rifles, as well as evaluations of the RPK-74 light machine gun, Alexander Arms' AR-platformed .50 Beowolf, Pro-Tech's automatic knives, a couple of Blackhawk's CQC holsters, and Bushnell's Stealthview II digital night vision monocular. There's always a Gear Locker section with brief product mentions of a variety of kit that a street cop can use. More importantly, product evaluations are honest, based on the authors' extended usage of the products, and they are never regurgitations of claims from manufacturers' product copy. Articles are thorough, well-paced, and steady in their focus, and they never feel dashed-out or chopped-off. Another big plus is the magazine's continuity. The articles usually continue onto following pages and are not cut off after the first page and spliced onto another page in the back.

My favorite sections are those about personal experience. "Frontline Debriefs" by Scott Reitz and "Training and Tactics" by Louis Awerbuck help to generate the warrior mindset, situational awareness, and a sense of self-criticism and humor. In the January issue, Awerbuck advocates training in no-light conditions to see where the user's basic gun-handling skills are, and, in a no-nonsense tone, he admonishes all of us who think we're above that "basic" kind of training. He's right, and those of us who have been there alone in the dark, juggling a pistol, a flashlight, and a radio with two hands, will be nodding our heads in both agreement and after-action-debrief embarrassment. Awerbuck writes, "Oh well, life isn't fair. Fair is a word that the state uses for an annual event wherein they sell candy floss and Ferris wheel rides. That's what fair is."

I don't have any complaints about _S.W.A.T._, but I can foresee some readers being turned off by the regular coverage of AR-style rifles. Guess what? Law enforcement largely uses the AR-platform, so this directly addresses the needs of the audience, and _S.W.A.T._ evaluates several brands of top-quality, rugged, affordable ARs and leaves other brands conspicuously out. Sorry, Colt. Sorry, Bushmaster. Sorry, Smith & Wesson. One other reviewer on Amazon did not like Leroy Thompson. He can write well, and he covers subjects in his field of specialty, protective services, so I don't see the reasoning there. I only wish Thompson would use more specifics when he throws us an anecdote from his vast experience, as he tends to include only general details about his training personal tactical teams and dignitary protection teams. I want to know specifics on the "certain European protective detail" that he will invariably bring up in his articles. I want to read Thompson's war stories. Is that too much to ask?

Both men and women are part of _S.W.A.T._'s audience; the magazine does not condescend to women by discussing a pink object in a token women's article. Training articles regularly feature men and women, and I have not come across a single instance in the writing that suggests that women are not equals to their male counterparts, unlike another cop magazine. As best as it can, _S.W.A.T._ remains nonpolitical as well as being ethnicity-neutral, but it still has a distinctly American voice. As for LE management, the magazine doesn't present much except for articles on training topics and tactical schools, but management isn't _S.W.A.T._'s intended audience. The suits and supervisors have enough of their own micromanagement publications, anyway.

I recommend _S.W.A.T._ to anyone involved or interested in law enforcement. It would make a great gift to your beat partner or the LE relative who never tells you what he or she would like for a birthday or holiday surprise. Amazon's price of $35 for a year's subscription is the going rate, and the twelve issues are definitely worth it. I re-subscribed for the next three years, which I got for a bargain at just under $90. Quite simply, this is the best law enforcement publication available.
top_rant.title: The Best Law Enforcement Magazine Available

top_rant.text:
Want closeup photos of people exploding out of deep powder with fine snow crystals spraying everywhere, pics of cute ski bunnies, and photos of desserts or appetizers? How about a periodical that focuses on trendy terms like "vert," "avy" and "brah?" If you said yes, SKI is your magazine. Want photos showing the entire mountain or its views, as well as articles containing facts, statistics and useful information about ski resorts and equipment? Well, keep looking, SKI Magazine is too cool and sophisticated to bother with such pedestrian fare.

I would think that at least 50% of the reason most people would subscribe to SKI Magazine would be to obtain information on ski resorts for planning a skiing vacation. SKI Magazine is remarkably useless at providing the sort of information a real person, as opposed to some urban hipster marketing industry person who has never been on a mountain, would want to read about. In the magazine's annual best ski resort issue (the first issue of the season, mailed out in September), which ranks the top 50 resorts, most of the photos show a closeup of the aforementioned skier exploding out of powder or show some other closeup of a skier on snow, with no reference at all to the location. Is he in Colorado, Utah, British Columbia, Austria, Vermont...? Maybe even in the UP of Michigan? We can't tell - there's no background usually shown. Do most people base their vacations on where one can most likely find cute chiquitas with huge smiles wearing ski outfits? Apparently the folks at SKI Magazine think so - they always include several such photos as the solitatry photo for a particular place in their resort issue. The September 2013 (for the 2013-14 season) resort guide features a photo of a slice of chocolate cheesecake for one resort. Gee, I was thinking that a photo of the mountain views might help steer me in the right direction on picking a ski vacation destination, but no, how silly of me - that slice of cake is much more relevant.

The typical skier would like to see all of the following stats for each resort:

-Base and summit elevations
-Vertical drop
-Number of trails and total size in acres
-Breakdown of difficulty of trails (# of beginner, intermediate, expert)
-Number and type of lifts
-Annual snowfall and percentage of trails covered by snowmaking
-Season (e.g. November 20th to April 15th, weather permitting)
-Lift ticket cost

Out of the 50 resorts they boast of on the front cover of their resort issue (actually only 40 in the 2012-13 best resorts issue - in SKI Magazine math 30 Western resorts + 10 Eastern resorts = 50 total resorts), they gave vertical drops on maybe five resorts and about the same with annual snowfall or size in acres. They give no stats at all for at least half of the resorts, instead focusing on on-slope or resort area dining or how remote or cold its location is. About the only helpful information that the resort guide typically provides is information on any expansion (added lifts, new trails, new lodge facilities, increased snowmaking, etc...) a resort has made since the last season or maybe a comment on how the resort ranks for families or off-slope activities. In other issues, longer articles on ski resorts generally omit half or more of the list of basic stats, instead focusing for half a page on some new restaurant at the resort and its brilliant Swiss chef or the environmentally-conscious ruminations of the resort's new manager, which would be material information for somewhat fewer than a dozen people in the entire world in deciding what resort to ski for their next vacation.

I'm not sure how reliable their rankings are for resorts, anyway, since they are allegedly based on surveys from "over 10,000 SKI subscribers." For example, the allegedly #5 ranked ski resort in the East is Holiday Valley in New York. Could people genuinely have felt this 750' hill is a better resort than Stowe, Killington, Sugarbush and Whiteface, all of which rank below it? I've skied Holiday Valley and it never would have occurred to me that it would even be in the top 20 in the rankings of Eastern ski resorts, let alone the top-5. Perhaps the resort hired a firm to stuff the balot box, as marketing companies do for book and other product reviews on this and other websites.

Equipment reviews tend to contain so many generalized terms that they don't really help. They don't quantify anything, either based on actual figures that could quantify performance objectively, or on say, a more subjective 1-10 scale for the testers to use to rate particular characteristics. The magazine takes care not to ever say anything negative about any ski for fear of displeasing an advertiser. The only conclusion one can draw from reading SKI Magazine reviews of skis is that they are all good, since they don't provide any information to the contrary. On the topic of candor and negativity, they did say that Killington has been mismanaged in recent years but did not describe how or why - that is the only negative comment I can recall appearing about any advertiser in the past two years.

As for positives, well, the skiing techniques and tips tend to be pretty good, though - more clearly written and easier to understand than, say, the golf instruction articles in Golf or Golf Digest magazine. The beginning of each issue usually does contain some pretty amazing photos of mountains and skiers (although often only accessible by helicopter) that get one wishing for the first big snowfall. And finally, one can obtain subscriptions (6 issues/yr) pretty cheap from a variety of sources - I think I got a 3-year subscription from a magazine discounter for something like $12.

Overall, the negatives outweigh the positives with this magazine. The perspective tends to be about 50% Madison Avenue uber-cool junior advertising executive ("the on-slope bistro's saffron creme brulee is TO DIE FOR!"), 45% jaded semi-pro ski bum looking for fresh, back country double black diamond runs (Brah! Get out of your yurt and let's go munch some fresh pow!"), and about 5% regular, typical skier.

EDIT 9/14: Well, the September 2014 issue came out and its equipment guide now does use a numerical rating system for skis and boots, including both an overall rating and a value rating, although the latter appears to be somewhat arbitrary. There is a disclaimer at the end of the article, though, stating that all of the skis were good, so no advertiser's feelings get hurt. However, we have a continuation of the hipster language with a reference to "groms" in an article on teaching kids to ski (is a "grom" supposed to be hipster for "kid?"). And we have more arrogant assumptions without explanations - in a guide to back country skiing, there's a section on avalanche airbags, without ever mentioning what exactly they do (I looked one up on REI's website and they act much like flotation devices in water, keeping a skier on the top of the avalanche instead of being buried). An article on the Steamboat resort remains annoyingly free of useful statistics, as usual. Plus it seemed like more than half of the magazine was taken up by special advertising sections extending many pages for several ski resorts, meaning one has to wade through a whole lot of pages just to get to the magazine itself. So while the new equipment rating system is a step in the right direction, the rest of the magazine continues to emphasize attitude and advertisers at the expense of providing useful, informative information.

EDIT 3/2/16: I just reduced the rating for this magazine to 1/5 star. I did this for three reasons:

1) For subscription year 2015-16, they sent me a grand total of 3 issues. Either they omitted a bunch or have scaled back so thoroughly that they have literally halved the number of issues and thus halved the value of the product. I think it's therefore fair to halve my rating.

2) Although they are getting better than they were in 2014-15 and prior years, the magazine still features a significant number of model-skiers wearing hats, not helmets. This is reprehensible. Last year I rode a lift with a guy who pointed to the spot on the slope where a beginner had crashed into his best friend a dozen years ago, the spot where there was an icy patch, and the specific tree that his best friend had slid into head-first, splitting open his skull and killing him. He applauded my good sense in wearing a helmet and said that if his friend had been wearing one, he'd be alive and on the lift next to him that day instead feeding worms in some cemetery. This year I was on a lift that stopped for about a minute, 50 feet over a beginner (one could tell by her short rental skis) who didn't think she needed a helmet. I looked down, saw all the blood where her head hit a tree, and saw the Ski Patroller desperately trying to administer CPR to keep her alive, and realized he was probably just trying to keep her organs functioning enough to harvest them for organ donation - she was clearly dead. Yes, this is very sobering. It was rather traumatic for my elementary school-aged kid to see, also. The problem is that the general public thinks snow is soft (unlike asphalt for a helmet-free motorcyclist, the risk involved in skiing are not self-evident), no one at all at the ski resorts is adequately promoting helmets or describing the dangers and risks of this great sport, and then we keep seeing all these fabulous, glamorous, and insanely adventurous skiers in magazines who eschew a helmet for a trendy, colorful hat. Ski Magazine should be ashamed of itself for not making it mandatory to be wearing a helmet if a model wants to appear in the magazine. Neither my daughter nor I will ever forget the dead young woman we saw this year. I doubt many, if any, people at this magazine have any appreciation whatsoever for what is actually involved in skier safety and they are so badly dropping the ball by failing to promote helmets.

3) In all of the issues I've ever read over the years, Ski Magazine has never once addressed the issue of skier safety. That's right, they have never once published an article (at least not in the past 3 years or any other years I've subscribed) that addresses any aspect whatsoever about skier safety. Let's be blunt, the entire ski industry has lawyered-up and legislator-ed up like virtually no other industry, and as a consequence, no players in this market are doing anything other than the very bare minimum needed to avoid liability (for resorts, that means minimal compliance with the recreational immunity statute that protects them from suit by injured skiers, in the form of tiny, never-read warning print on their waiver forms). The only entity that I've seen in the ski industry that actually has taken any affirmative steps to promote skier safety is Breckenridge, which has very prominent signs up on a number of their lifts that serve intermediate, advanced and expert terrain "NO BEGINNERS." Ski magazine could serve as a valuable forum for promoting safety. Instead of some horse manure article on which resorts now have restaurants serving Pho (Vietnamese noodle soup), promote instead resorts that are going out of their way to keep people from getting injured. If there was a resort that adopted a mandatory helmets policy (see #2 above), promote and praise that resort and soon others would follow. Ski Magazine could take a leadership role in the ski industry in promoting safety and saving lives, but it does absolutely nothing, preferring to be driven by marketing concerns, not the concerns of genuine skiers.

EDIT 12/22/16: The January 2017 issue just arrived and it is crammed with articles and features that suggest to me that the magazine is run by people who neither know much about skiing nor care about the sport. I'll preface these critical comments by listing the positive first and stating that this issues article on Snowbird did list some important stats and did focus on a lot of relevant information about upgrades the resort has done or will complete in the next few years, so this was a marked improvement over most resort articles of the past few years. However, the issue also contained an article (one of several of a continuing series on automobiles) on full-sized SUVs that get better gas mileage. I don't know about you, but a ski magazine is just about the last place I'd consider as a source for what new vehicle to purchase. I'm pretty sure not a single person has ever sworn at how old, worn-out, in need of repair, etc... his or her car has become and then, behind the wheel or staring at it in the middle of the garage, suddenly said "Wait a second! I know where I'll turn for advice on my new ride - Ski Magazine!!"

The magazine's content continues to suggest that someone high on its editorial board came over from a culinary or wine magazine with no experience at all with skiing - they insist on trying to shoe-horn articles or prominent portions of articles about resorts on the topic of food and drink, in lieu of more relevant information about the actual skiing experience at those resorts. At least in the January 2017 article, it appears the author actually attended the particular food and wine event at Beaver Creek about which she was writing. Most of these articles that fill up pages of every issue do not seem to reflect that anyone from the magazine personally sampled the food mentioned in the articles, but could have been based on responses and photos supplied by resorts' marketing directors to inquiries about promoting the resorts' food and beverage options. People do take gastro-vacations, but their destinations are places like New Orleans, New York, San Francisco and the wine country, Chicago, etc.... Maybe a few dozen families each year take a resorts' restaurants into serious consideration when they plan a ski vacation.

That portion of the magazine that is created by genuine skiers continues to dedicate its focus to expert skiers. The cover story for this month's issue is skiing Alaska and almost all of the article is on heli-skiing, of interest really just to experts. Ski also used a ski-hipster term in that article that was new to me, derogatorily referring to "Herbs," I suppose to indicate non-experts or, people like me who do not know what a "Herb" is. The page on the Bear Mountain section of Killington is devoted to mogul runs. This focus is akin to car magazines devoting 70% of their reviews to 600hp supercars that 99% of their readership cannot afford.

Here's what I'd like to see included in this magazine, beyond what I've outlined above:

- At least one monthly article devoted to family ski trips - focus on what resorts have a good quantity of beginner and intermediate terrain, which have good kids skiing programs and instruction, which resorts have abundant nearby off-trail activities for that one family member who isn't as avid of a skier.

- A monthly instructional article aimed at transitioning skiers from the "can somewhat comfortably ski blues" to the competently handle black diamond terrain level of skill. The substantial majority of skiers fit someplace in or near this range of ability.

- Focus on skier safety. Every issue should have at least a half page devoted to skier safety and warning of the dangers of the sport.

- Every article on a resort should contain at least one panoramic photo of the main portion of the resort and a trail map with full mountain stats.

- More articles on skier fitness, particularly in the earlier issues. I skied probably 20 days at my local Midwestern ski area last year before a trip to Colorado and thought I was in pretty good ski shape, but was rather shocked at how my thighs felt like they were on fire going down 2000+ vertical feet of trails. I definitely would have done some exercises beforehand if I had a bit better warning of what to expect and some sort of instructional articles to read.

- A monthly article dedicated to the budget-minded skier or ski family. Have multiple tips about how to enjoy this great-but-expensive sport more economically. Not some hipster/internet hero/semi-comedic tale of crashing in the back of a VW van or in some ski hippy's teepee while dumpster-diving for dinner, but advice on resorts that give the best bang-for-the-buck, how and when to get discounted lift tickets or airfares, how to save on equipment and gear, etc.... I'll start by giving an example: on your next trip to Colorado, consider Loveland instead of the better known resorts. You get at least 100% of the views, scenery, and challenging terrain of the more prestigious resorts, at least 80% less crowds, their lift tickets can be frequently had discounted for about 1/3 of the cost of a Breckenridge or Vail lift ticket, and if one buys a Loveland season pass, it costs about what 3 days at Vail or Beaver Creek would cost and includes 3 days of skiing at Crested Butte, too.
top_rant.title: 1/5  Lots of Marketing Fluff, Little Substance

top_rant.text:
29 August 2015

First of all, readers should know that Scientific American is not a product originating in the United States.

It has been owned by a German publisher since 1986.

The editors frequently note that Scientific American is owned by Nature Publishing Group (since 2008), but that in turn had been owned by the same German publisher as part of a purchase of yet another company, Macmillan, in 1995. There is nothing about Scientific American related to the United States of America other than it was originated in the United States in the 19th century.

I started reading Scientific American shortly before the change occurred.

In my opinion Scientific American has over a period of years gone from at least a minimum of science articles to nearly 100% consumer conditioning, pseudo-science, and misinformation by design. Like all magazines, Scientific American profits from advertising. One might expect at least some sincere attempts at elucidating science, given the title. But that would be an unrealistic expectation.

The range of misinformation appearing in Scientific American these days includes long debunked race schema rants to religion rants (more on that below). I can no longer recommend this magazine to anyone. In the distant past there were actual science articles, by design. Now if any science gets into the magazine it is completely by random error, not by design.

It is only due to those few examples of insight with practical value that I look through the magazine for useful content, but no naive reader not well educated in the alleged subjects presented within its covers would be able to discern the ruse.
______________________________
When I first began reading Scientific American prior to 1980, I was inspired to set a goal for myself to be able to understand the science articles. So I set out to educate myself with a broad foundation in the fundamental paradigms of science. Scientific American shortly thereafter set out a goal to go in the opposite direction, deciding to misinform for profit, and in a very blatant manner.
______________________________
I do not mean to advertise the two editors noted below (Michael Shermer, Steve Mirsky) or change this review of Scientific American to a critique of either of them, but rather to use them as examples of the anti-intellectual and anti-science paradigm of Scientific American.

Michael Shermer has posed as a scientist for many years but really has no knack for it and does not appreciate the nature of science at all. He seems to be under the impression that atheism and science are the same thing, and that critical reasoning and science are the same thing, too.

But atheism and science are completely unrelated, and critical reasoning in and of itself does not add any content to science.

It is no surprise that he is more inclined to ruminate on religion and to otherwise promote pseudo-science: for at least part of his career he was a professor in a private Christian college, and apparently not required to undergo peer review from real scientists in his chosen specialty--psychological science--of which he has demonstrated little to no knowledge or insight.

Steve Mirsky specializes in attempting to vainly appear more intelligent and knowledgeable than he really is by pointing out that some selected target of derision is stupid. When he cannot accomplish that, he writes filler consisting of a seemingly endless stream of puns, but no science content at all. He also holds and from time to time promotes a race-religion schema in complete contradiction to biological science. In particular, he appears to believe that Jewish religion is transmitted genetically.
______________________________
As a typical example of gross incompetence and worse, consider the December 2012 Scientific American wherein Michael Shermer wrote some ad copy and as usual presented it as an alleged book review, conveniently for a product of the parent company: "The Wisdom of Psychopaths". The title itself should give readers the general idea. He was dead serious, as usual.

In another book promotion--his specialty--he attempted to qualify the content of the book by repeating a claim from its author that only human beings exhibit cooperative behavior, narrowing it down to some unrealistic ad hoc constraints. But anyone familiar with animal behavior knows that there are numerous examples of cooperative behavior among and between other animal species.

One might expect that a man with PhD in psychology would have learned that by now from standard texts of comparative psychology--also known as animal behavior--but he did not know, or simply chose to mislead the readers, or both. The result is the same: the magazine is spreading pseudo-science by design, not by random error.

In his previous life, before becoming an editor at Scientific American, he was a born-again-Christian activist. I think he still is but has since claimed to be an atheist. Not that it makes any difference. There is no reason to believe that an atheist has any more insight into science than anyone else.

science (noun): The intellectual and practical activity encompassing the systematic study of the structure and behavior of the physical and natural world through observation and experiment, and the data and conclusions founded on that activity.

Atheism, by contrast, just means that some person does not believe that there exists one or more alleged gods in the real world.
______________________________
Like the video infotainment industry, Scientific American magazine is in the business of consumer conditioning, i.e., advertising and propaganda. It only poses as a science journal. And also like the video infotainment industry, there is no way Scientific American will publish anything that might conflict with the goals of their clients, be those multinational corporations or governments who pay for the content.

That has not always been the case but it is now the guiding rule for content at Scientific American.

Two other examples follow, both of a generalized nature.
______________________________
Scientific American and the Psychiatric Drug Industry

It is not uncommon for Scientific American to include in some otherwise mundane and unrelated article a statement to the effect that "we hope our research will help us better understand schizophrenia and other genetically transmitted mental illnesses." I do not mean to imply that that exact wording has been used, but the message has been used frequently and without challenge.

The consumer conditioning aspect for their corporate clients is obvious. They are pushing Big Pharma's disease model of mental health to basically sell quack medicines for imaginary mental illnesses invented by the American Psychiatric Association, a trade group that profits from it in a quack like manner.

Given the goals of the multinational corporate clients of Scientific American, those sort of embedded claims are meant to promote what amounts to witchcraft and magic potions with no clear benefit to the consumer. That may not be obvious to those of us who live in countries where witch burning, mandatory religion, and child sacrifice are not common. But that is not the case everywhere and there are plenty of gullible, under-educated people who will believe just about anything if it is presented in an alleged science journal.
______________________________
Scientific American and the Nuclear Power Industry

In the past, when there was a different editor and editorial board, Scientific American was proud to announce that it had predicted the catastrophe that arrived as 2005 Hurricane Katrina: "Drowning New Orleans: In a harrowing prediction of what would become the future, this 2001 feature notes that a major hurricane could swamp New Orleans under 20 feet of water, killing thousands" by Mark Fischetti.

Keeping that in mind, directly after the 2011 Fukushima (Japan) disaster, wherein a tsunami disabled a nuclear reactor, releasing radioactive material that has made some areas of Japan into ghost towns--and which is still leaking radioactive pollution into the ocean the last time I checked for news--the only comment that Scientific American had was that we need to convince the general public to believe that nuclear power is safe. By "we" I suppose the editorial management was describing their directive from corporate management, an internal matter that would be obvious enough from the nature of their articles promoting allegedly new and improved reactor designs.

But where I live (California, USA) if either the San Onofre reactor (closed but stocked with spent fuel) or Diablo Canyon coast-based reactor is breached by a tsunami, a similar outcome can be expected. Tsunamis are not predictable except by one metric: they happen along coastlines. We know they will probably occur sooner or later, we know where, but we do not know exactly when.

That was of no concern to the editors at Scientific American (nor to current California Gov. Jerry Brown or President Barack Obama, for that matter) because it would be contrary to the interests of their corporate sponsors to consider that cogent message. Likewise it is of no concern to the Japanese government corrupted by the manufacturers and operators, nor to the Chinese government which is corrupt from the top down, and both of which still promote coastal location of nuclear reactors.

So what is the problem? Climate science related to Hurricane Katrina is far more complicated than the geography that assures us that underwater seismic events cause tsunamis and are inevitable. But not even the 2004 Indian Ocean tsunami event, and again, the Fukushima disaster, has convinced the editors at Scientific American that there is an inherent safety problem with coastal siting of nuclear reactors.

Scientific American will still report on the aftermath of the Fukushima disaster and its effect on the environment, rendering some places unlivable and affecting the genetics of at least some species of fauna. But there is no way Scientific American will take a stand on the ill-advised propensity of nuclear power operators to site reactors on coastlines.
______________________________
There are many similar problems with the credibility of Scientific American. A great deal of the magazine's article content is no better than someone selling snake oil to ignorant and gullible persons. It is clear that promoting science is not now and probably never will again be a mission of Scientific American. They are not out to educate readers; they are out to manipulate readers for profit.

Don't waste your time on it if you want to learn more about science.

Your best bet would be to acquire overstock or used college texts online for a few dollars and start educating yourself.
top_rant.title: Let the buyer beware.

top_rant.text:
Nearly four years ago, I bought a two-year subscription to The Economist. This newspaper-magazine is supposed to be the most serious and highbrow analysis of the previous week's events, aimed specifically at the university-graduate market (unlike the popular weeklies such as Time, Newsweek, etc.) Yet unfortunately, this magazine has proved extremely disappointing to me.

The Economist has a reputation of being a 'kinda' right-wing, but mostly centrist magazine (endorsing Bill Clinton in 92 and 96 but George W. Bush in 2000 rather than a leftish populist like Al Gore, while going back to endorsing John Kerry in 2004) that can be counted on for being very pro-capitalistic no matter what.

The other negative reviewers think this magazine is a 'rightist' magazine purely because The Economist strongly supports a form of globalization: globalization being the great bogey-man of the anti-capitalist left. I am going to show you why The Economist is not only not a conservative magazine, but is essentially a mouthpiece for the morality and economics of the Left. In other words, this magazine is not only leftist according to John-Birchers (not hard), but it is leftist even according the Republican party.

The Economist makes no pretentions of value-neutrality in its reporting (which is good, I don't believe there can be such a thing, so why hide it?) so what you get is very editorialized lead articles, subsection articles (which form the bulk of the magazine), and book reviews; all pervaded with the values of the Economist's reporting staff.

This magazine is quite open about the social and political positions it adopts, unlike say the New York Times and the Washington Post (officially unbiased and neutral, but unofficially the epitome of left-liberal), so you better make sure your values and ideas about your country and society coincide with the staff of the Economist before you invest a penny in a subscription.

As to The Economist's view of moral questions, The Economist is the very embodiment of anti-conservative bias and prejudice. It is extremely secular humanist in its general view of the world; it supports abortion on demand for all nine months of pregnancy as a basic right for women; it supports widespread contraceptive use and population 'control' (euphemism) measures in Asia and Africa; stem-cell research on aborted fetuses and fetuses intentionally grown; it advocates legalized prostitution and the moral indifference of sexual promiscuity; they believe the best way to stop Africa AIDS is to flood Africa with condoms; they insist homosexuality is perfectly natural and normal and possibly morally superior (any deviation from that party-line is homophobia, a word they like to throw around--a lot) and are loudly in favor of the civil recognition of homosexual unions as authentic marriages; they opposes the death penalty for murderers and rapists; endorse easy-divorce laws; defend compulsory taxpayer-financed schooling; Milton Friedman-style school vouchers; show anxiety and enmity towards America's homeschooling phenomena; strongly defends socialist national health care systems(!!!); university educations that are mostly socialist (and that education is a right the state must provide at socialized collective expense); big strong governments (i.e. governments involved with economic central planning); big strong standing militaries and interventionist foreign policies in search of foreign monsters to slay; pro-Iraq War; supra-national governmental agencies like the U.N., N.A.F.T.A. and the International Court of Justice which trump national congresses or parliaments; is a hard-core supporter of religious pluralism and always defends the view that the central tenants of traditional Christianity are absolutely false and the tenants of secular humanism true (which I guess is nice if you are a secular humanist).

'Fundamentalists' in the US (translation: adherents of pre-1920s style Christianity), admittedly an eccentric and varied group, both amuse and scare the writers of the Economist and they are often the subject of editorials in the American section, ones that paint them mercilessly in negative terms. The writers have great difficulty mentioning Christians who haven't gutted their beliefs with theological liberalism without using the word 'zealot' (check for it, 'zealot' and 'Christian' appear together as often as 'conspiracy' and 'theory') and delicately though straight-forwardly hinting that bible-believers are dangerous simpletons who are also bigots. The Economist argues that Fundamentalists dominate the Republican party, to the Republican party's great shame. Does any of this sound very conservative to you?

As to its view of political economy or economics, the staff of the magazine has their good points. They oppose and lampoon outright leftist protectionist boondoggles like France's "industrial champion" policy and Britain's former policy of "picking winners". They dislike erecting tariff-barriers in the face of outsourcing or alleged foreign 'dumping', and are pretty good at mocking various featherbedding rules. The Economist is always a friend to the inescapable logic of Ricardo's theory of comparative advantage; a foe to protectionism of all (i.e. most) stripes and flavours. Great! But unfortunately... the Economist also has a lot of problems with its economic prescriptions.

The magazine has writers on it who demonstrate various economic tendencies: Keynesians, Chicagoites, Historicists, and other positivists. The common thread is statist-economics. Nixon said in the seventies that we're all Keynesians now, and the description continues to aptly fit The Economist. It is solidly neo-classical as opposed to new classical. It is relentlessly in favour of so-called counter-cyclical action by every nation's Central Bank. It views business cycles as an inevitable defect of the capitalist system which has absolutely proven the necessity of the welfare state. It insists monetary policy (inflate the money supply!) and fiscal policy (deficit spending!) are the right recipes for the welfare state to 'smooth out' the bumps of the business cycle, and I guess as Keynes said, "save capitalism from itself." They argue the Great Depression of the 1930s 'proved' the folly of laissez-faire capitalism and the requirement of a "third way" that mixes the alleged best features of socialism with the best features of capitalism while supposedly avoiding the worst of both (a.k.a. the Keynesian "revolution" in economics). They fully reject the gold monetary standard and embrace on principle fractional reserve banking, central banking and fiat money as alone desirable. They are hard-core defenders of the welfare-state, and a large activistic government. The Economist pokes fun at Germany and Japan's moribund economies and sclerotic labour markets, but you know that as Keynesians & Chicagoites all they really want for these two countries is for them to operate more like the U.S.A.'s government, so the criticisms are pretty tame since of course the U.S. government is huge, and keeps on growing.

Prospective conservative subscribers should be growing apprehensive by this point, I hope.

Where once the classical and neo-classical (pre-Keynesian) schools proclaimed the value of laissez-faire and a market free from state intervention, today's mainstream (post-Keynesian/general equilibrium) economists maddeningly find more and more justifications for the state to be getting involved due to all sorts of putative market failures. Unquestionably, the economic and finacial advice this magazine provides is far from what the laissez-faire economists of the classical/early-neoclassical (Smith, Ricardo, Say, Mill, Jevons) and Austrian (Menger, Bohm-Bawerk, Von Mises, Rothbard) schools taught. I find that utterly annoying and grating. While still controversial to suggest it, I think the entire economics profession has shifted to the left over the last century. At any rate: the Economist is squarely mainstream Ivy League statist economic orthodoxy. If you are the sort of person who thinks abolishing the income tax might be a good idea, you are already way, way too far to the right for this magazine, you crazy nut-case.

Something an economics-literate person might ask themselves is do they believe FDR's New Deal shortened or prolonged the Great Depression of the 1930's? If they think shortened, then the Economist might be for them, and it will try to reinforce that belief. But if you are a conservative and suspect the creation of the welfare state might not have been very good, then the Economist will just grate on you, week after week, since it will tell you it was VERY good.

I let my subscription to the Economist expire without renewing. So much for that experiment. Please don't make the same mistake I made in thinking this was a conservative magazine.
top_rant.title: Very Disappointing Magazine for Conservatives

top_rant.text:
Update on March 10, 2017: Once again I'm changing my rating, this time to 2 stars. Also I cancelled my subscription a few months ago. Political bias (anti-Trump, pro-Clinton) is obvious in at least 2 issues of RD: July/Aug 2016, p.96-100, "Which Way Do I Vote?"; Nov 2016, p.102-111, "Who They Really Are."

Update on Jan 25, 2016: I'm changing my rating of Reader's Digest (again) because I no longer feel that it deserves 5 stars. I'm torn between 3 and 4 stars, but will be generous (for now) and give it a 4 stars because it continues to feature some good articles and I like its handy size.

What is downgrading the magazine for me is the proliferation of ads and articles for diet books written by RD editor-in-chief, Liz Vaccariello. Liz Vaccariello has written a multitude of diet books and cookbooks in recent years--at least 7 diet books and at least 5 cookbooks. (If you think I'm exaggerating the number of her books, look her up on Amazon.) What I don't like is that she regularly promotes her books in Reader's Digest, with frequent ads and articles. Apparently she did the same thing when she was editor of Prevention Magazine, but I was never a regular reader of that magazine, so I don't know much about that. Also Prevention is a diet and exercise magazine, unlike RD.

The Feb 2016 issue of RD features an article on Liz's latest diet book. There were also 3 ads for same book in that issue--1 inside the article, another elsewhere in the magazine, and the other on the back of the mailing address card. I wouldn't complain about this if it were a rare event, but it's not: Liz's diet books are regularly promoted and advertised in RD. Being the editor of a high-circulation magazine gives her a great advertising advantage, no doubt. It's a sweet deal for her, but I find her continuous self-promotion to be annoying (and obviously very biased).

By the way, is there any evidence that her diet books work long-term? I'd love to see an article in Reader's Digest titled "Secrets Diet Book Authors Won't Tell You", but I won't hold my breath on that. I don't care how much weight Liz or her staff and friends claim they lost on her latest diet after following it for days, weeks, or a few months. I won't be impressed until there is proof that the weight loss is being maintained long-term by many. As we have all heard countless times, strong evidence indicates that the great majority of people regain whatever weight they lost on any given diet within 1-5 years. And their health often declines in the process. And it's not their fault, either: Diets (including those promoted as simple, easy, and healthy) are generally complicated, unpleasant (for the dieter and their families), costly, and are not realistically sustainable long-term. But regardless of the quality or effectiveness or lack thereof, I am simply tired of seeing Liz's diets promoted so often.

As someone else mentioned: Since my 2014 update, RD went back to 10 issues per year. The decrease in issues doesn't bother me as long as the expiration dates don't mysteriously change as they did a few years ago. On a positive note, RD is now including digital access with each subscription. While I don't plan to take advantage of that myself, it's an added value which I expect will be popular with many readers.

Update on Aug 18, 2014: My original review below was written almost 2 years ago. I'm happy to say that there have been many improvements since then, so I now need to update my review and change my rating from 1 star to 5 stars. Everything I complained about in my original review has been corrected! The magazine continues to publish occasional "mature" jokes, but on a mild level--nothing offensive like that dirty joke about the Easter Bunny. There are now 12 issues per year (1 per month) with no more confusing and changeable expiration dates. Another improvement: the number of ads was actually reduced. Also the quality of the magazine's paper has improved. No more thin, slippery paper; now the paper is thicker and whiter, which makes it easy on the eyes as well as easy to turn the pages. Best of all, the articles have significantly improved! Lately every issue has been full of interesting, informative, and heartwarming articles. My congratulations and thanks to editor Liz Vaccariello and the other staff for all these great improvements. I very happily resubscribed to Reader's Digest!

Original Review (Nov. 15, 2012): Until recently Reader's Digest had all-clean jokes. You could read all the jokes out loud to your children or grandchildren of any age. No more. Page 110 of the September 2012 issued featured a joke about the Easter Bunny having sex with a chicken. Isn't that a great joke for your children and grandchildren? This joke didn't accidentally sneak into the magazine. They knew the joke was dirty, which is why it was titled "Favorite joke I can't share with my kid." In another comedy section on page 121 of the same issue, you get to see an x-ray of a man that has sunglasses up his rectum (due to accidentally sitting on them). Not exactly family-friendly humor.

Many customers (myself included) wrote to editor Liz Vaccariello (she became editor in 2011) to complain. In the Letters section of the November 2012 issue, Ms. Vaccariello apologized for the "off-color joke" about the Easter Bunny (which she calls "a rabbit") and said, "Please know it won't happen again." Yet in that very same November issue there are additional off-color jokes. For example, on page 160, there's a joke about a woman who punches her husband for "25 years of bad sex" and he punches her back for "knowing the difference."

I'll mention something else new (in the past couple of years) that I don't like about the magazine: the expiration dates keep changing. Occasionally they put out what they call a "special" issue, which they count as 2 issues. When this happens, your expiration date automatically changes. If at first it expires in May, then it's changed to April, then to March. On and on it goes and it's even more annoying when it involves a gift subscription. Let's say you give a subscription to a friend for a Christmas gift. You paid for "one year" and expect your friend to receive issues from Jan 2013 through Dec 2013. Well, that won't happen because there will probably be 2 "special" issues during 2013. In that event, your friend's subscription will automatically be changed to expire with the Oct 2013 issue. Of course, Reader's Digest will start bugging you to renew your gift subscription well before it expires. So around June, they will try to get you to renew what's supposed to be a Christmas gift. If you renew it that year, come the following year, the new expiration will likely have been changed to August. Have fun trying to keep up with Christmas gifts when you have to renew them in the summer. Reader's Digest pulls the same expiration date stunt on all the wonderful Reiman magazines (Reminisce, Country, Birds & Blooms, etc.). The Reiman magazines are still high quality, thanks to having good editors, however Reader's Digest has made some unwelcome changes to them, as well--most regrettably, the addition of ads in these formerly ad-free publications.

Well, that's it for me. Today (after subscribing for about 30 years) I cancelled my subscription to Reader's Digest.
top_rant.title: Anti-Trump Liberal Bias

top_rant.text:
[Wednesday 2020-03-11] Update: Amazon just sent me another renewal notice for Reason Magazine (Print + Kindle) despite the fact that I have never received a single issue of Reason in the mail from Amazon. Every time I attempt to contact them about the issue, the

I do not think I have received a single copy of Reason via the USPS in recent months, if ever, since my subscription to Reason [Print + Kindle] was supposed to go active. I did cancel said subscription as soon as I found that Amazon has a perverse policy of insisting that customers accept auto-renew when they subscribe. I think when I first wrote this message, I managed contact Amazon customer support and was asked to spend a lot of time checking my Kindle compatible devices to see if it was there. I do not give a damn about the Kindle version, since I prefer to read the print edition magazines such as Reason -- which is a great magazine, by the way.

The one start rating I gave above is due only to the incompetence of Amazon.com. I have bought and thoroughly enjoyed several issues of Reason at Barnes and Noble over the past couple of years I know my mailbox can get full of junk mail and other magazine format publications, but surely Amazon could have sent me at least one issue of Reason by snail mail, as they promised me a whole year of them.

In the future, if I subscribe to anything from Amazon, I will select just the edition on physical media. Kindle-only magazine subscriptions are useless in ordinary circumstances, as far as I am concerned.]

Anyone with a clue would be crazy to want to default to auto-renew (on a subscription to anything). Why? Well, now I am being bombarded with spam asking if I want to renew, which is what I expected after being told the subscription would not expire until the end of the (one year) period had expired. But at least I have not been billed for another year of a [Print + Kindle] magazine where I do NOT receive the print version.

I have several copies of Reason magazine I purchased at Barnes and Noble a few miles from here over the past several months and have NOT received a print copy in the mail from Amazon. I really do not care for Kindle except as a reference tool or for times and places where carrying around printed publications (including books, especially hardcovers) is inconvenient. For a magazine I generally read from cover to cover, it is delivery of the print copy that matters to me.

There may be Kindle editions of recent issues of Reason are on my computer or one of the several other Kindle-compatible devices I own, including desktop and laptop computers, laptops, tablets, and smartphones.

Amazon makes it an incredible PITA to communicate with a customer representative by phone which is what most intelligent people I know consider a major sign of poor customer service, so I was glad I cancelled my subscription to Reason [Print + Kindle] when I was told that would be the only way to avoid being "auto-billed" (read: suckered into paying more) for another year. It was not worth my time and effort to try and get the matter corrected because I do not recall if I ever received, much less read Reason in Kindle format in 2019. I did read many issues I paid for at Barnes and Noble and other physical bookstores because they showed up as I organized my magazine collection in the process of committing a neatness ("Spring Cleaning") so that I could put all the 2019 issues of Reason and other magazines on yet another set of (still in box) bookshelves I need to build to make more shelf space.

Reason magazine is great and I have been making a point of getting a print copy of each new issue for years when I am in a brick and mortar bookstore that carries it. I often end up with duplicate copies which I usually give to friends who happy to get them but have returned if I catch my mistake soon enough to still have the receipt available.

Reason covers a wide range of topics well and tends to be very informative an usually entertaining. I just checked my Kindle subscriptions on this laptop computer and found no issues. There may be some on the one Kindle device I have or on any of the machines/devices around here that can load Kindle eReader. I really do not care as without the print copies, the Kindle edition is not worth subscribing to if one likes to read magazines and books printed on dead tree products or large digital displays, an mostly for reference even then.

In summary: Reason magazine is wonderful and I have enjoyed reading the paper edition since the 1990s (1980s?).

Amazon subscription service is something to be avoided, in my opinion, yet I DO like Amazon.com a lot, overall. I learned that there is a brick-and-mortar Amazon store two or three miles from here and have yet to visit it, but I may be able to get this subscription to the [Print + Kindle] edition of Reason straightened out with a human customer service representative there when I go to check the place out.

I should point out that when it became clear to me that going completely deaf in one ear with hearing replaced by loud tinnitus in that ear made Audible subscription products rather useless for me, I had little problem getting Audible subscriptions cancelled, twice, because I subscribed to something on Audible again (on impulse or out of habit) before I remembered that it would end up being hard to hear well. That was back before it became as difficult as it is now to get through to Amazon by phone and bypass the annoying bot that tries to keep you away from a person you can actually converse with in a normal manner.

I would love to have all the print issues of Reason [Print and Kindle edition] I did not get because I am sure I missed at least two or three months of the print edition in 2019 when did not drive to a bookstore every month-- it is hard to tell because I do not keep every issue, especially when I thought I would have it available on Kindle as a backup or reference copy. I avoid throwing any issue of a publication that is less than a year old to avoid the duplicate issue problem that occurs when I have not gotten around to binge reading the last few months of some subscriptions.

Another caveat: I know a lot of tech-savvy people who loved Kindle to keep up with their regular reading and used various kinds of optical enhancement equipment to read medical and other professional journals they enjoyed long after they retired from active practice. When a portable eReader is my only option due to failing eyesight or more situational reasons, I will probably end up using and liking eBooks for purposes other than reference a lot more.
top_rant.title: Reason: is great!!  --  Amazon Subscription Service: tends to be  lame!

top_rant.text:
Unlike competitor [[ASIN:B00006KYFC Fanfare]] magazine, American Record Guide (or ARG) has published classical music criticism continuously since the 1930s making it more a peer to England's [[ASIN:B00LY55SQQ Gramophone]] magazine. Also, American Record Guide dedicates plentiful pages of every issue to music in performance in America and elsewhere. This includes sections called concerts everywhere and another that focuses on opera. Fanfare provides no information on concertizing, but both publications -- which come out six times a year in the same size booklet sized magazine -- focus mostly on reviewing new classical recordings provided to the editors from manufacturers and distributors. ARG has even been known to review recordings provided from its readers.

I once thought ARG had an edge over other publications in terms of quality reviewing but I no longer believe that. This is, in the main, because many of its best critics have either died or left the magazine and the ones that followed them -- usually from universities, performers, teachers or similarly from the classical music establishment and not experienced music journalists -- are not as good as the ones they replaced. Also, their critics tend to be specialty critics and write reviews in one area.

There are exceptions, the most notable being Roger Hecht, an experienced critic that is the remaining star of ARG's roster. John Barker, a Baroque critic, is also about the best critic in the world in his area and several others -- Bill Gatens, another Baroque critics, Ralph Lucano who reviews opera, David Radcliffe who reviews historic recordings, Paul Althouse who reviews Romantic music, Alan Gimbel who reviews modern music and Mark Koldys who reviews piano and film scores among them -- are as good or better than other critics in other magazines. Steve Haller, another reliable generalist critic with a broad repertory, writes less now than I've known in 15 years subscribing to the magazine. I thought he went over to Fanfare at one point.

ARG's best critics know the history of classical recordings and are never overwhelmed by new artists, recordings or the flavor of the month. They are able to put these into perspective for the reader and compare them to recordings and artists from the past, perhaps better than in other magazines.

However, I find a growing blandness in the reviews I didn't find a year or three or five years ago, in part because some many the best critics have retired, died or left the magazine. One of its very best, John McKelvey, an expert in 19th century Romantic music, died in 2014. Also, the editor, Donald Vroon, has taken on a larger role reviewing recordings.

Vroon is a controversial figure in music criticism and is, at best, a paraprofessional critic. Where this defeats ARG most greatly is Vroon only reviews well-known music that everyone knows, depriving his readers of quality reviews on recordings of top 200 works. He had been around musicians many years and traveled widely yet it's clear from his reviews he has never performed music and has no understanding of music from a performance perspective.

His reviews are often laughably brief when he dismisses something he doesn't like. In these reviews (and he is not the only member of the cast that writes them), ARG becomes more akin to the two- or three-sentence reviews you might read on Amazon that essentially say "I love it!" or "It sucks!" No one should pay money for such reviews yet this magazine publishes them.

Vroon is an interesting figure in music but, sadly, an overwhelmingly negative force. He is among the throng that believe the world is going to hell in a hand basket, in his case because he thinks society it is in cultural decline. Anything he doesn't like shows up in the magazine somewhere and he authors harangue after harangue about what he says he "can't stand" like television, airport security, period performance practice and lite beer.

He recently published a book full of these diatribes that ARG has shamelessly promoted and -- again out of character for this publication -- one of its best critics wrote a lengthy softball review of the book. Had someone other than Vroon written that book, ARG's critics would have eviscerated it in writing. Vroon is also occasionally anti-intellectual and borderline racist.

On the plus side, ARG regularly reviews 21st century music in a feature called The Newest Music. I don't know another classical music magazine that regularly carries a section on 21st century music -- though I've known many that waste space on jazz, Bollywood and other non-classical interests.

I'd like to address three other things about ARG: cost, modernism and overview. First, cost: an ARG publication costs less than Fanfare and just about every other classical music publication and still delivers 200-300 pages of reviews and information with very little advertising. By contrast, Fanfare carries up to 600 pages per issue. However, many of the extra pages in Fanfare are front end interviews and reviews that endorse the plentiful pages of color advertising before the main reviews begin. There was 300 pages of this stuff in the last issue I read.

Second, modernism. ARG is about as old time as a publication can be -- white paper, black ink, two columns of text, almost no art and its webpage carries only a PDF of the current and immediate past issue available only to subscribers. By contrast, Fanfare, Gramophone and [[ASIN:B00006K5EF BBC Music Magazine]] all carry a searchable database of older reviews (at Fanfare only available to subscribers) and Gramophone has a searchable database where you can find reviews in almost any issue of the magazine ever published. ARG is hopelessly outdated here -- but it costs less, too.

Last is overview, the greatest feather in ARG's hat. An overview is a special 10-15 page article that summarizes reviews of all recordings from a composer's entire discography, for a portion of a composer's production, or by group such as film composers, German opera, or English orchestral music. Sadly, ARG doesn't publish these very often anymore. You can still collect overviews by purchasing older hardcopy issues; it would be far more convenient if you could read them online or download them from the ARG website.

ARG proudly proclaims itself an independent publication that carries little advertising and is free of record promotion and hype. This is untrue, of course; all publications that cover classical music are part of the hype machine for that industry, ARG included. However, ARG is the only publication that carries no inane interviews with musicians and no slick advertising urging you to rush out and buy new recordings.

The truth is most classical music publications don't like to be critical of new releases. Neither do most critics. Some magazines, like the newest -- [[ASIN:B0058EM902 Listen : Life With Classical Music]] -- don't even attempt to be critical of new releases. When I read ARG, I know they are looking at every release they publish with a critical eye, sometimes even a critical eye jaundiced by a form of critical yellow fever. So, for its growing shortcomings, it still has a certain value.
top_rant.title: Still my favorite but slipping

top_rant.text:
If you didn't notice, Country Living has now turned into a BRAND NAME plastered all over crappy items in KMart so as to compete with Martha Stewart's junk in the next aisle over. As a magazine, CL is now a showcase for product placement with conspicuous ads on the facing page, or worse, feature pages telling you how to get the same look, like as many lame fashion mags who teach awkward teens how to mimic the exact outfit their favorite celebrity wore. The only women who actually do that are those who don't understand that replicating another woman's outfit is only meant for Halloween. And the only women who do that with their home are catalog imitators. If Country Living wants to be a catalog, please note stores give them out for free, and I see more ''Country Living'' in Pottery Barn these days than I do in CL!

It is also ridiculous the disproportionate number of 30-to-40-somethings featured in the "articles" who all seem to live in and around NYC, and all seem to be about the age of the CL staff... hmmm do I smell nepotism? Or has CL just become a homogenous blog of For-NY-By-NY narcissism? All these people coincidentally have the sponsored Ikea and Kmart products (that haven't even come out yet) just laying about their houses too. Nothing is organic anymore. Nothing is about decades of collecting, or family heirlooms, or really anything authentic whatsoever. It's all just a photo shoot at the editor's buddy's house where they get the sponsor "samples" free while the rest of America foots the bill for these yuppies who have no taste.

I watched a re-run of HGTV's ''Design on a Dime'' today; a show featuring VERY low-cost decorating ideas for kids fresh out of college, or adults who seem to have lost control of their home. My cable detail showed the re-run was from 2008, and a project featured was making shelves out of cardboard molds used to make concrete columns. This SAME project was JUST featured in a 2011 issue of CL. How insulting. That is CL's brilliant advice? Regurgitated 3-year-old projects from a low-rent design TV show? I never viewed CL as a magazine for people on a budget. In light of the US economy in recent years I get that we all need to hold back a little, but those who can afford a magazine subscription can probably afford materials better than cardboard to enlist as shelving for their walls. It really feels like the cheaper element on etsy.com threw up all over the pages for the benefit of the editor's fellow 20-30-year-old Brooklynites who make up the coffee-getting staff in the office, and need tutelage to someday become the editorial dept who gets the 2-page spread of IKEA in their upstate buyout of some poor farmer who they think they will imitate with ''classy'' box store products.

Admirers of these low-end projects are those same 20-30-year-old Brooklynites (or their ilk in other cities) who spend 90% salary on clothes and entertainment and wonder why they have nothing left over for a high-rent look in their loft... so they resort to cheap trendy cardboard for a fast and easy thrill that will last as long as their latest hook-up.

The new Country Living magazine is NOT for you if:

A.) You are not a friend, family member, or suck-up to the editorial staff at CL
B.) You are over the fresh-out-of-college lifestyle and are wanting to decorate with materials more suitable than cardboard
C.) You are able to afford real furniture from places nicer than IKEA
D.) You are even slightly knowledgeable of real antiques and vintage items, and actually have these types of furnishings in your home
E.) You are capable of craft projects that use more than the tools found in preschool, such as a hammer and nails, and real wood
F.) You understand that ''Country Living'' as a lifestyle requires elements of country (as in the realities of rural culture, horticultural experience, and pastoral custom) that one can't mimic from a perch in a Manhattan highrise filled with beadboard

Last thought, has anyone noticed the full-page ads in the back for Amish made fireplaces? Is that a JOKE?!?!?! That is the most disgraceful hot mess of nonesense I have ever seen! I can't tell whether to laugh or cry. Amish people don't allow their photos to be taken, yet these cheesy ads show actors in full costume filing wood planks with beaming smiles... and women would not be working alongside the men like that. Plus, the way it is worded, such that the Amish are suffering from the recession and need the work so they are producing these stupid fireplaces at dirt cheap prices--who does that appeal to? Who out there is looking to take advantage of poor Amish people? Are these the people buying Country Living now? EWW!!! Country Living deserves to go bankrupt for running that ad alone! The commercialism and sell-out quality BS that fills the rest of the pages is further proof that they now officially suck. I want my money back.
top_rant.title: Turned into a HOT MESS nighmare!
